| Gini importance of feature $j$ | $\text{VI}_j = \sum_{t \,:\, \text{split on } j} p(t)\,\Delta i(t)$ | Mean decrease in impurity |
| Permutation importance | $\text{PI}_j = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^B \left[\text{err}_{b}^{\text{perm},j} - \text{err}_{b}^{\text{orig}}\right]$ | Mean decrease in accuracy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Random Forests are deployed in **medical diagnosis** (gene-expression classification), **remote sensing** (land-cover mapping), **credit scoring** in finance, **fault detection** in industrial IoT, and **cybersecurity** (intrusion detection). Their robustness to noise, handling of mixed data types, and built-in feature importance make them a go-to model in production systems where interpretability (via variable importance) and accuracy are both required.

## 2.5 Bagging vs Random Forest — Key Differences

| Aspect | Bagging | Random Forest |
|---|---|---|
| Introduced | 1994 (Breiman) | 2001 (Breiman) |
| Randomness source | Bootstrap samples only | Bootstrap + random feature subsets |
| Base learner | Any (trees, SVMs, etc.) | Decision trees only |
| Tree correlation | Moderate | Low |
| Variance reduction | Partial | Strong |
| Computational cost | Lower | Higher (still parallelizable) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Expected Unique Fraction in a Bootstrap Sample

**Question.** Given a dataset of size $n$, what fraction of *unique* instances do we expect to retain when we draw $n$ samples with replacement?

**Step 1 — Probability a single specific instance is NOT picked in one draw:**
The probability of selecting *any one particular* instance in a single draw is $\frac{1}{n}$. Hence the probability of *not* selecting it is $1 - \frac{1}{n}$.

**Step 2 — Probability it is not picked in any of the $n$ draws:**
Since draws are independent, the probability of never being selected across all $n$ draws is:

$$P(\text{not picked}) = \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Probability it IS picked (i.e., it appears at least once):**
$$P(\text{picked}) = 1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$:**
Using the famous limit $\lim_{n \to \infty}(1 - 1/n)^n = 1/e$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} P(\text{picked}) &= 1 - \frac{1}{e} \\
&= 1 - 0.3679 \\
&\approx 0.6321
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** $\approx 63.2\%$ of unique instances appear in any given bootstrap sample; the remaining $\approx 36.8\%$ form the **Out-of-Bag (OOB)** set.

## 3.2 Derivation: Variance of an Averaged Ensemble

**Setup.** Let $\hat{f}_1, \hat{f}_2, \dots, \hat{f}_B$ be $B$ predictors with identical variance $\sigma^2$ and pairwise correlation $\rho$. Define the ensemble prediction:

$$\hat{f}_{\text{ens}} = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b$$

**Step 1 — Expand the variance using $\text{Var}(aX) = a^2 \text{Var}(X)$:**

$$\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{ens}}) = \text{Var}\!\left(\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b\right) = \frac{1}{B^2} \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b\right)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the variance-of-sum identity:**

$$\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b\right) = \sum_{b=1}^{B} \text{Var}(\hat{f}_b) + 2 \sum_{1 \le b < c \le B} \text{Cov}(\hat{f}_b, \hat{f}_c)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute identical variances and identical pairwise covariances:**

$$\text{Cov}(\hat{f}_b, \hat{f}_c) = \rho\,\sigma^2$$

The number of unordered pairs is $\binom{B}{2} = \frac{B(B-1)}{2}$. So:

$$\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b\right) = B\sigma^2 + 2 \cdot \frac{B(B-1)}{2} \cdot \rho\sigma^2 = B\sigma^2 + B(B-1)\rho\sigma^2$$

**Step 4 — Divide by $B^2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{ens}}) &= \frac{B\sigma^2 + B(B-1)\rho\sigma^2}{B^2} \\
&= \frac{\sigma^2}{B} + \frac{(B-1)\rho\sigma^2}{B} \\
&= \rho\sigma^2 + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{B}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**
- As $B \to \infty$, the second term vanishes, leaving a *residual* variance of $\rho\sigma^2$ governed entirely by tree correlation.
- This is why **Random Forest's feature-subset randomness** is so critical: it aggressively drives $\rho \to 0$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem.** Consider a classification task with $M = 16$ features, $n = 1000$ training instances, and $B = 100$ trees. Compute: (a) default $m$ for classification, (b) OOB sample size, (c) expected OOB error if 360 of the 1000 OOB predictions are misclassified.

### Part (a) — Default $m$ for classification:
$$m = \sqrt{M} = \sqrt{16} = 4 \text{ features per split}$$

### Part (b) — Expected OOB sample size per tree:
$$\text{OOB size} \approx 0.368 \times n = 0.368 \times 1000 = 368 \text{ instances}$$

### Part (c) — OOB error:
$$\text{err}_{OOB} = \frac{360}{1000} = 0.36 \;\;(36\%)$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Gini Importance Computation

For a single tree, suppose feature $X_j$ is used at three internal nodes with the following properties:

| Node $t$ | Samples reaching $p(t)$ | Impurity drop $\Delta i(t)$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.40 | 0.30 |
| 2 | 0.25 | 0.20 |
| 3 | 0.15 | 0.10 |

**Total Gini importance for $X_j$ in this tree:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{VI}_j &= \sum_{t=1}^{3} p(t)\cdot \Delta i(t) \\
&= (0.40)(0.30) + (0.25)(0.20) + (0.15)(0.10) \\
&= 0.120 + 0.050 + 0.015 \\
&= 0.185
\end{aligned}$$

Across the forest of $B$ trees, the average importance is $\overline{\text{VI}_j} = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \text{VI}_j^{(b)}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bagging Workflow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Bagging. Explain how it reduces variance in decision tree models.**

**Model Answer:**
Bagging, short for **Bootstrap Aggregating**, is an ensemble technique proposed by Leo Breiman (1996) that trains multiple base learners (typically decision trees) on different bootstrap samples of the training data and combines their predictions via voting (classification) or averaging (regression). A *bootstrap sample* is a sample of size $n$ drawn *with replacement* from the original dataset of size $n$.

Bagging reduces variance because decision trees are *high-variance* learners — small perturbations in data lead to structurally different trees. By averaging $B$ such trees, the variance shrinks as $\sim \frac{1}{B}$ (for uncorrelated trees). Crucially, since each tree sees a slightly different dataset, the *errors* of individual trees are partially decorrelated, so averaging them cancels out noise while preserving their low-bias signal. The bias of the ensemble is approximately equal to the bias of a single tree, so the net effect is reduced generalization error.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**What is the role of random feature selection in Random Forests? How does it differ from pure bagging?**

**Model Answer:**
In a Random Forest, at every split of every tree, only a **random subset of $m$ features** is considered as candidate splitters, where $m \ll M$ (total features). Typically $m = \sqrt{M}$ for classification and $m = M/3$ for regression.

The role of this randomness is to **decorrelate the trees**. In pure bagging, if a single feature is overwhelmingly strong, every tree will pick it as the root split, producing highly correlated trees — which limits variance reduction. By restricting each split to a random feature subset, *different trees* are forced to use *different features* at their upper nodes, producing structurally diverse trees.

This additional randomness yields a **lower residual correlation $\rho$** among trees, and hence a **lower ensemble variance** than pure bagging. The cost is a small increase in bias, but the net effect is a stronger model.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **Bagging algorithm in detail** with a clear step-by-step procedure, suitable diagram, and discuss its **advantages and limitations**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **expected fraction of unique instances** in a bootstrap sample of size $n$ drawn from a dataset of size $n$. Use this result to explain **Out-of-Bag (OOB) error estimation**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Bagging Algorithm (7 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):** Bagging (Bootstrap Aggregating) is an ensemble method proposed by Leo Breiman (1996) that combines $B$ base learners, each trained on a bootstrap sample of the original data, by averaging (regression) or voting (classification).

**Algorithm (3 marks):**
1. Input: training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^n$, number of iterations $B$, base learner $\mathcal{A}$.
2. For $b = 1, 2, \dots, B$:
   - Draw a bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}_b$ of size $n$ by sampling from $\mathcal{D}$ *with replacement*.
   - Train a base model $\hat{f}_b = \mathcal{A}(\mathcal{D}_b)$.
3. Output the ensemble:
   - **Regression:** $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} \hat{f}_b(x)$.
   - **Classification:** $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \arg\max_{y} \sum_{b=1}^{B} \mathbb{1}(\hat{f}_b(x) = y)$.

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
- Reduces variance without increasing bias significantly.
- Effective on unstable learners (decision trees, deep networks).
- Parallelizable across trees.
- Provides built-in OOB validation.
- Robust to outliers and noise.

**Limitations (1.5 marks):**
- Loss of interpretability (ensemble is a "black box").
- Computationally expensive for large $B$ or large data.
- Can underperform boosting on some problems.
- May not help with stable, low-variance base learners (e.g., k-NN, Naive Bayes).

---

**(b) OOB Derivation (7 marks)**

**Derivation (4 marks):** *See Section 3.1 above for full step-by-step working.*

- Probability a particular instance is not selected in one draw = $1 - \frac{1}{n}$.
- Probability it is not selected in any of the $n$ draws = $\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n$.
- Probability it IS selected at least once = $1 - \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n$.
- Taking the limit $n \to \infty$: $\lim_{n \to \infty}\left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right)^n = 1/e \approx 0.368$.
- Therefore the expected unique fraction retained is $1 - 1/e \approx 0.632$ and the **OOB fraction** is $\approx 0.368$.

**OOB Error Estimation (3 marks):** The OOB instances for tree $b$ are those not present in its training bootstrap $\mathcal{D}_b$. We use these to predict each instance's class:

$$\hat{y}_i^{OOB} = \text{mode}\{\hat{f}_b(x_i) : i \notin \mathcal{D}_b\}$$

The OOB error is then:

$$\text{err}_{OOB} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\bigl(y_i \neq \hat{y}_i^{OOB}\bigr)$$

This serves as a **free internal cross-validation**, comparable to a 3-fold CV estimate.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **Random Forest algorithm** in detail. Compare it with **Bagging** and explain how random feature selection controls **tree correlation and variance**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With $M = 25$ features, $n = 2000$ samples, and $B = 200$ trees, calculate the default number of features $m$ considered at each split (classification), the expected OOB sample size per tree, and the variance reduction factor $\rho + (1-\rho)/B$ assuming $\rho = 0.15$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**(a) Random Forest Algorithm (7 marks)**

**Algorithm Steps (3 marks):**
1. For each tree $b = 1, \dots, B$:
   - Draw a bootstrap sample $\mathcal{D}_b$.
   - Grow an unpruned CART decision tree on $\mathcal{D}_b$.
   - At each node, randomly select $m$ features from $M$ total.
   - Choose the best split among only these $m$ features.
2. Predict by majority vote (classification) or mean (regression).

**Comparison with Bagging (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Bagging | Random Forest |
|---|---|---|
| Feature randomness | None | Random $m$ at each split |
| Typical base learner | Any | Decision trees |
| Tree correlation | Higher | Lower |
| Variance reduction | Moderate | Strong |

**Variance Control (2 marks):** The variance of an averaged ensemble of $B$ correlated predictors is:

$$\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{ens}}) = \rho\,\sigma^2 + \frac{(1-\rho)\sigma^2}{B}$$

By forcing each split to use only $m = \sqrt{M}$ features, Random Forest **reduces $\rho$** substantially. If a dominant feature exists, in pure bagging every tree will split on it first, but in Random Forest, that feature appears in the random subset only $\sim \sqrt{M}/M = 1/\sqrt{M}$ of the time, so trees diverge.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks)**

**Default $m$ for classification (2 marks):**

$$m = \sqrt{M} = \sqrt{25} = 5 \text{ features per split}$$

**Expected OOB sample size per tree (2 marks):**

$$\text{OOB size} = 0.368 \times n = 0.368 \times 2000 = 736 \text{ instances}$$

**Variance reduction factor with $\rho = 0.15$, $B = 200$ (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{ens}}) / \sigma^2 &= \rho + \frac{1-\rho}{B} \\
&= 0.15 + \frac{1 - 0.15}{200} \\
&= 0.15 + \frac{0.85}{200} \\
&= 0.15 + 0.00425 \\
&= 0.15425
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A single tree has variance $\sigma^2$, but the ensemble reduces this to $\approx 15.4\%$ of $\sigma^2$ — an **84.6% reduction** in variance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing OOB fraction with test error:** OOB is an *internal* estimate, not a substitute for a held-out test set in production. State this clearly.
> 2. **Forgetting to "with replacement":** Bootstrap *requires* replacement; sampling without replacement gives a different technique (subsampling) with different properties.
> 3. **Using $m = M$ for Random Forest:** This collapses Random Forest into bagging. Always emphasize the constraint $m \ll M$.
> 4. **Misquoting the 63.2% / 36.8% rule:** These are *limits* as $n \to \infty$. For small $n$, compute exactly using $1 - (1 - 1/n)^n$.
> 5. **Averaging class labels in classification:** Always use **majority vote**, not numerical averaging of class labels.
> 6. **Skipping the bias-variance discussion:** In KTU's 14-mark questions, the examiner allocates ~3 marks specifically for the *why* — always connect your answer to bias-variance decomposition.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅

- 📌 **Bagging = Bootstrap + Aggregating** — proposed by Breiman (1996).
- 📌 **Random Forest = Bagging + Random Feature Subset** at each split — proposed by Breiman (2001).
- 📌 **Bootstrap** is sampling *with replacement*; on average **63.2%** unique instances are retained, **36.8%** form OOB.
- 📌 **Default $m$ for classification:** $m = \sqrt{M}$; **for regression:** $m = M/3$.
- 📌 **Aggregation rule:** **mean** for regression, **majority vote** for classification.
- 📌 **OOB error** provides a free internal test estimate ≈ 3-fold CV accuracy.
- 📌 **Variance formula:** $\text{Var}(\hat{f}_{\text{ens}}) = \rho\sigma^2 + (1-\rho)\sigma^2/B$. Reducing $\rho$ is the key.
- 📌 Random Forest **trees are NOT pruned** — fully grown trees maximize the decorrelation benefit.
- 📌 **Gini Importance** (Mean Decrease in Impurity) and **Permutation Importance** are the two main feature-importance measures.
- 📌 Random Forest is **robust to outliers**, handles **mixed data types**, requires **no feature scaling**, and is **parallelizable**.
- 📌 Key limitations: loss of interpretability, large model size, slower inference than single trees.
- 📌 Exam favourite: always expect a 14-mark question linking **bagging ↔ bias-variance decomposition ↔ OOB derivation**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unsupervised - Principal Component Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET527 - INTRODUCTION TO MACHINE LEARNING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Unsupervised - Principal Component Analysis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** is a **statistical, unsupervised, linear dimensionality-reduction technique** that orthogonalizes the data by projecting it onto a new set of uncorrelated axes called **Principal Components (PCs)**. These axes are ordered so that the **first principal component (PC1)** captures the **maximum variance** in the data, the **second principal component (PC2)** captures the **maximum remaining variance** orthogonal to PC1, and so on. Mathematically, PCA performs an **orthogonal linear transformation** of an *n × d* data matrix **X** into an *n × k* matrix **Z**, where $k \le d$, such that the columns of **Z** (the principal components) are linearly uncorrelated and retain as much of the original variance as possible. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Terminology Key Points to Memorize** > - Unsupervised → **No labels required** during training. > - Linear → Each PC is a **linear combination** of original features. > - Orthogonal → Successive PCs are **statistically uncorrelated** (mutually perpendicular). > - Variance Maximization → PCs are directions of **maximum spread**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Shadow on the Wall" Intuition) Imagine a **3D cloud of glowing fireflies** floating in a dark room. You want to capture their shape using only a **2D photograph** (a wall). The wall is fixed, but you are allowed to **rotate the cloud** to any angle before casting its shadow. - The **best rotation** is the one that gives the **widest, most spread-out shadow** — because a wide shadow preserves the most information about the original shape. - PCA finds **exactly this best rotation** automatically, and the two axes of the wall become the **first two principal components**. - The **brightness (variance)** along each axis tells you how much "information" that axis is preserving. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Analogy — Camera Sensor** > In digital imaging, every pixel captures Red, Green, Blue (3 features). PCA is used in image compression (think **JPEG / Eigenfaces**) to find the most important combinations of colors. A 1000-dimensional pixel vector is reduced to 50 principal components, retaining ~95% of the information. --- ## 1.3 Why PCA is Used in Machine Learning | Engineering Problem | Role of PCA | |---|---| | **Curse of Dimensionality** | Reduces *d* features to *k* (k ≪ d), making distance metrics meaningful. | | **Visualization** | Projects high-D data to **2D/3D** for human inspection. | | **Noise Filtering** | Low-variance directions often correspond to noise and can be discarded. | | **Feature Decorrelation** | Produces **uncorrelated features** for downstream models (regression, clustering). | | **Computational Efficiency** | Smaller feature space → faster training, lower memory footprint. | | **Multicollinearity in Regression** | Removes correlated predictors, stabilizing linear models. | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** PCA is **not feature selection** (it does not pick a subset of original features). It is **feature extraction** — it **creates brand-new features** that are linear combinations of the originals. --- ## 1.4 Geometric & Statistical Meaning of a Principal Component A **principal component** is a unit vector **w** of length *d* such that when the centered data is projected onto **w** (i.e., the scalar projection $z_i = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w}$), the **sample variance** of the resulting scalars $\{z_1, z_2, \ldots, z_n\}$ is **maximized**. For the $j^{th}$ principal component, the direction $\mathbf{w}_j$ is the **eigenvector** corresponding to the $j^{th}$ **largest eigenvalue** $\lambda_j$ of the **covariance matrix** of the data. The variance captured by $\mathbf{w}_j$ is numerically **equal to $\lambda_j$**. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of PCA PCA is a **linear method** built upon four key assumptions: 1. **Linearity** — The data lies near a linear subspace. 2. **Mean and Variance are Sufficient** — Only first and second-order statistics (mean, covariance) are used; higher-order moments (skewness, kurtosis) are ignored. 3. **High Variance Implies High Information** — Directions with the largest spread carry the most "signal" (a foundational but debated assumption). 4. **Orthogonality of Components** — PCs are mutually perpendicular (uncorrelated). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard PCA fails for non-linear manifolds.** For such data, use **Kernel PCA**, **t-SNE**, or **UMAP** (these are *not* in the KTU 2024 syllabus for this module, but worth knowing). --- ## 2.2 The PCA Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic Given a data matrix $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ with $n$ samples and $d$ features: | Step | Action | Mathematical Form | Why? | |------|--------|-------------------|------| | **Step 1** | **Mean-center** the data (and optionally standardize). | $\mathbf{x}_i \leftarrow \mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$ | Translates origin to data centroid so PC1 passes through the center of mass. | | **Step 2** | Compute the **d × d covariance matrix** $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. | $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X}$ | Encodes pairwise feature variance and covariance. | | **Step 3** | Compute **eigenvalues** $\lambda_1, \ldots, \lambda_d$ and **eigenvectors** $\mathbf{w}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{w}_d$ of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. | $\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}_j = \lambda_j \mathbf{w}_j$ | Eigendecomposition finds orthogonal directions of variance. | | **Step 4** | **Sort** eigenvalues in descending order: $\lambda_1 \ge \lambda_2 \ge \cdots \ge \lambda_d \ge 0$. | — | Ensures PC1 has the highest variance. | | **Step 5** | Choose top $k$ eigenvectors → form the **projection matrix** $\mathbf{W}_k = [\mathbf{w}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{w}_k]$. | $\mathbf{W}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}$ | These $k$ columns span the best $k$-D linear subspace. | | **Step 6** | **Project** the centered data onto this subspace. | $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{W}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k}$ | Yields the $k$-D representation. | > [!WARNING] > **KTU 2024 Common Mistake:** Forgetting to mean-center the data **before** computing the covariance matrix. If you skip Step 1, PC1 will point toward the absolute mean of the cloud, not along the direction of maximum spread. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|----------|---------|---------------| | 1 | Sample mean vector | $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}_i$ | Vector of feature means | | 2 | Centered data | $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_i = \mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}}$ | Rows of $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ | | 3 | Covariance matrix | $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ | Symmetric, **positive semi-definite**, $d \times d$ | | 4 | Single entry of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ | $\Sigma_{jk} = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\tilde{x}_{ij})(\tilde{x}_{ik})$ | Diagonal entries = variances | | 5 | Eigen decomposition | $\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{W} = \mathbf{W} \mathbf{\Lambda}$ | $\mathbf{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1,\ldots,\lambda_d)$ | | 6 | Variance of $j^{th}$ PC | $\text{Var}(\mathbf{Z}_j) = \lambda_j$ | Eig. value = variance captured | | 7 | Projection to k-D | $\mathbf{Z} = \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{W}_k$ | Reduced representation | | 8 | Reconstruction (back to d-D) | $\hat{\mathbf{X}} = \mathbf{Z} \mathbf{W}_k^T + \bar{\mathbf{X}}$ | Approximate original data | | 9 | Reconstruction error | $J = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i \Vert^2$ | Squared Frobenius norm / *n* | | 10 | Total variance | $\text{Tr}(\mathbf{\Sigma}) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j$ | Sum of eigenvalues | | 11 | **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)** | $\text{EVR}_j = \dfrac{\lambda_j}{\sum_{m=1}^{d} \lambda_m}$ | Fractional importance of PC *j* | | 12 | **Cumulative EVR (for selecting k)** | $\text{CEVR}(k) = \dfrac{\sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j}{\sum_{m=1}^{d} \lambda_m}$ | Choose smallest $k$ such that CEVR $\ge$ threshold (e.g. **0.95**) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symbolic Note for KTU Answer Sheets:** Always use $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ (uppercase sigma) for the covariance matrix to distinguish it from the summation symbol $\sum$. Examiners will penalize confusion. --- ## 2.4 Choosing the Number of Principal Components *k* There are **three** standard methods, all commonly asked in KTU exams: 1. **Cumulative Explained Variance Threshold** — Choose the smallest $k$ such that $\text{CEVR}(k) \ge 0.90$ or $0.95$. 2. **Scree Plot (Cattell's Criterion)** — Plot $\lambda_j$ vs. $j$ and look for the **"elbow"** point. Select $k$ at the elbow. 3. **Kaiser Criterion** — Retain all PCs with $\lambda_j \ge 1$ (only valid for data standardized to unit variance). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Real-World Utility:** In **face recognition** (Eigenfaces, Turk & Pentland, 1991), 3000-pixel face images (≈3000 features) are routinely reduced to $k = 50$–$100$ PCs, achieving >95% variance retention and a 30×–60× speedup in classification. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Why the Principal Component Maximizes Variance **Goal:** Find a unit vector $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ (i.e., $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$) that maximizes the sample variance of the projections $\{z_i = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w}\}$ of the **centered** data points. ### Setup We assume the data is already centered: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0}$. The projection of sample $i$ onto $\mathbf{w}$ is the scalar $z_i = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w}$. ### Step 1: Write the sample variance of the projections The sample variance of $\{z_i\}_{i=1}^{n}$ is given by: $$ \text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (z_i - \bar{z})^2 $$ Because $\bar{z} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_i \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}^T \mathbf{w} = 0$ (centroid at origin), the expression simplifies to: $$ \text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (z_i)^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w})^2 $$ ### Step 2: Convert to matrix form Recognize that $(\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w})^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w}$. Substituting: $$ \text{Var}(z) = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w} $$ Since $\mathbf{w}$ is a constant (does not depend on $i$), it can be factored out: $$ \text{Var}(z) = \mathbf{w}^T \left( \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbf{x}_i \mathbf{x}_i^T \right) \mathbf{w} $$ The bracketed term is precisely the **sample covariance matrix** $\mathbf{\Sigma}$: $$ \text{Var}(z) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} $$ ### Step 3: Formulate the constrained optimization problem We wish to **maximize** $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ subject to the constraint $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$. Mathematically: $$ \max_{\mathbf{w}} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1 $$ ### Step 4: Use the method of Lagrange multipliers Define the Lagrangian: $$ \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, \lambda) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - 1) $$ Set the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ to zero (using the identity $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{w}$ for symmetric $\mathbf{A}$): $$ \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2 \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - 2 \lambda \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0} $$ ### Step 5: Arrive at the eigenvalue equation Simplifying: $$ \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w} $$ This is the **definition of an eigenpair** of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. Therefore, the direction $\mathbf{w}$ that maximizes variance is an **eigenvector** of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$, and the maximum variance is the **eigenvalue** $\lambda$. ### Step 6: Selecting PC1 To get the **maximum** variance, choose the **largest** eigenvalue $\lambda_1$ and its eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_1$. By the **spectral theorem for symmetric matrices**, the eigenvectors of the real-symmetric $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ are mutually orthogonal, guaranteeing that subsequent PCs (eigenvectors) are uncorrelated. > [!NOTE] > **Final result for the answer sheet:** > $\boxed{\text{PC}_j \text{ direction} = \mathbf{w}_j, \quad \text{where } \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}_j = \lambda_j \mathbf{w}_j, \quad \text{variance} = \lambda_j.}$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Total Reconstruction Error Formula **Goal:** Show that minimizing reconstruction error is **equivalent** to maximizing variance. ### Step 1: Define reconstruction Let the data be projected onto the top $k$ PCs in matrix $\mathbf{W}_k$. The reconstruction in the original $d$-D space is: $$ \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i = \bar{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{W}_k \mathbf{W}_k^T (\mathbf{x}_i - \bar{\mathbf{x}}) $$ ### Step 2: Define mean squared reconstruction error $$ J = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \Vert \mathbf{x}_i - \hat{\mathbf{x}}_i \Vert^2 $$ ### Step 3: Substitute Using the orthonormality $\mathbf{W}_k^T \mathbf{W}_k = \mathbf{I}_k$ and the trace identity $\sum \Vert \mathbf{x} \Vert^2 = \text{Tr}(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X})$: $$ J = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j $$ (Proof involves the identity that the sum of squared projections onto **discarded** PCs equals the sum of their eigenvalues — derivable via Pythagoras in the eigenbasis.) ### Step 4: Equivalence $$ \min_{\mathbf{W}_k} J = \min_{\mathbf{W}_k} \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \max_{\mathbf{W}_k} \sum_{j=1}^{k} \lambda_j $$ So **maximizing retained variance** ⇔ **minimizing reconstruction error**. QED. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-mark question) **Problem.** Given the centered 2D dataset $\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix}$ (already mean-centered). Compute the principal components. ### Step 1: Verify centering Sum of each column is $2+3+1+4=10$ and $1+2-1+3=5$, so the original data has mean $\bar{\mathbf{x}} = (2.5, 1.25)$. We use the centered matrix $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ given above. ### Step 2: Compute covariance matrix $\mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1}\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ First compute $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$: $$ \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 & 4 \\ 1 & 2 & -1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 3 & 2 \\ 1 & -1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 20 \\ 20 & 15 \end{bmatrix} $$ With $n-1 = 3$: $$ \mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 30 & 20 \\ 20 & 15 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 6.667 \\ 6.667 & 5 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 3: Solve $\det(\mathbf{\Sigma} - \lambda \mathbf{I}) = 0$ $$ \det \begin{bmatrix} 10-\lambda & 6.667 \\ 6.667 & 5-\lambda \end{bmatrix} = (10-\lambda)(5-\lambda) - 44.44 = 0 $$ $$ \lambda^2 - 15\lambda + 50 - 44.44 = 0 \Rightarrow \lambda^2 - 15\lambda + 5.56 = 0 $$ ### Step 4: Eigenvalues $$ \lambda = \frac{15 \pm \sqrt{225 - 22.22}}{2} = \frac{15 \pm \sqrt{202.78}}{2} = \frac{15 \pm 14.24}{2} $$ $$ \boxed{\lambda_1 \approx 14.62, \quad \lambda_2 \approx 0.38} $$ ### Step 5: Eigenvector for $\lambda_1$ Solve $(\mathbf{\Sigma} - 14.62 \mathbf{I})\mathbf{w} = 0$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} -4.62 & 6.667 \\ 6.667 & -9.62 \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{w} = 0 $$ From the first row: $-4.62 w_1 + 6.667 w_2 = 0 \Rightarrow w_2 = 0.693 w_1$. Normalize: $\Vert \mathbf{w}_1 \Vert = 1 \Rightarrow w_1^2 + 0.693^2 w_1^2 = 1 \Rightarrow w_1 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.481}} \approx 0.822$. $$ \boxed{\mathbf{w}_1 = (0.822, \ 0.569)^T} $$ ### Step 6: Explained variance ratio $$ \text{EVR}_1 = \frac{14.62}{14.62 + 0.38} = \frac{14.62}{15.00} \approx 0.975 = 97.5\% $$ So projecting onto PC1 alone retains **97.5%** of the variance — reducing 2D → 1D is fully justified. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 PCA Algorithmic Flowchart (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Raw Data Matrix X of size n x d] --> B[Step 1: Mean Centering
xi = xi minus xbar] B --> C[Step 2: Standardization
Divide by std if needed] C --> D[Step 3: Compute Covariance Matrix
Sigma equals 1 by n minus 1 times X transpose X] D --> E[Step 4: Eigen Decomposition
Sigma W equals W Lambda] E --> F[Step 5: Sort Eigenvalues
Descending order lambda1 to lambdad] F --> G[Step 6: Choose Top k Eigenvectors
Form Wk of size d x k] G --> H[Step 7: Projection
Z equals X times Wk size n x k] H --> I[Reduced k-D Representation Z] I --> J[Optional: Reconstruction
Xhat equals Z Wk transpose plus xbar] J --> K[Approximate Original Data Xhat] ``` ## 4.2 Geometric Interpretation Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ORIG[Original 2D Feature Space] P1[Data point x1]:::data P2[Data point x2]:::data P3[Data point x3]:::data P4[Data point x4]:::data end subgraph AXES[Principal Axes] PC1[PC1 direction w1
Max variance]:::pc1 PC2[PC2 direction w2
Orthogonal to PC1]:::pc2 end subgraph RED[Reduced 1D Space] Z1[z1 scalar projection]:::z Z2[z2 scalar projection]:::z Z3[z3 scalar projection]:::z Z4[z4 scalar projection]:::z end P1 --> Z1 P2 --> Z2 P3 --> Z3 P4 --> Z4 classDef data fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef pc1 fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#B22222,color:#FFF classDef pc2 fill:#4ECDC4,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000 classDef z fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Variance Retention Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Input: d eigenvalues lambda1 greater lambda2 ... lambdad] --> B[Compute Total Variance
Vtotal equals sum of all lambdai] B --> C[For each j compute EVRj equals lambdaj divided by Vtotal] C --> D[Compute Cumulative EVR
CEVRk equals sum first k divided by Vtotal] D --> E{Decision: CEVRk greater or equal threshold
Typically 0.90 or 0.95} E -->|Yes| F[Select k as optimal number of components] E -->|No| G[Increment k and recompute] G --> E F --> H[Output: Reduced k dimensional PCA representation] ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Pipeline: PCA in an ML Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw High-Dimensional Data
Images, Genes, Text] --> B[Preprocessing
Cleaning, Normalization] B --> C[Mean Centering] C --> D[Covariance Computation] D --> E[Eigendecomposition] E --> F[Select k Principal Components] F --> G[Transform Data to k-D] G --> H[Downstream Task
Classification or Clustering or Regression] H --> I[Model Evaluation
Accuracy, RMSE, Silhouette] I --> J{Tune k} J -->|Adjust| F J -->|Final| K[Deployed Model with PCA Pipeline] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define Principal Component Analysis. List any two of its key properties.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (2 marks):** PCA is an **unsupervised, linear dimensionality-reduction technique** that transforms correlated high-dimensional data into a smaller set of uncorrelated variables called **principal components**, ordered by the amount of variance they capture. > > **Two Key Properties (1 mark):** > 1. **Orthogonality** — All PCs are mutually uncorrelated (perpendicular in feature space). > 2. **Variance Maximization** — PC1 captures the maximum variance; PC2 captures the maximum of the *remaining* variance, and so on. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is the significance of eigenvalues in PCA? Mention the role of the scree plot.* **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Significance of Eigenvalues (2 marks):** Each eigenvalue $\lambda_j$ represents the **amount of variance** captured by the $j^{th}$ principal component. The **ratio** $\frac{\lambda_j}{\sum \lambda_m}$ gives the **Explained Variance Ratio (EVR)**, indicating the relative importance of that PC. > > **Role of Scree Plot (1 mark):** A scree plot is a line plot of eigenvalues $\lambda_j$ versus component index $j$. The optimal number of PCs is chosen at the **"elbow"** of the curve — the point where the rate of variance drop sharply levels off. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting mean-centering** before computing $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ (loses 1–2 marks). > 2. **Confusing PCA with Feature Selection** — PCA *creates* new features; it does not pick a subset of original ones. > 3. **Not stating that PCs are unit vectors** — always show the normalization step. > 4. **Skipping the orthogonality proof** in derivations — examiners expect you to *mention* the spectral theorem. > 5. **Wrong scaling** — failing to standardize features with different units (e.g., height in cm vs. weight in kg) inflates one eigenvalue artificially. --- ### Question A — Internal Choice Option 1 (14 Marks) **Q3. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** *Derive mathematically the principal components of a dataset using the eigenvalue decomposition of the covariance matrix. State and justify all assumptions.* **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Setup and assumptions (1 mark)** Assume data $\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is **mean-centered** ($\sum_i \mathbf{x}_i = \mathbf{0}$). Linearity, second-order sufficiency, and the variance-as-information assumption hold. **Step 2: Variance of projection (1.5 marks)** For a candidate direction $\mathbf{w}$ with $\Vert \mathbf{w} \Vert = 1$, the projection of $\mathbf{x}_i$ is $z_i = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w}$. The sample variance is: $$ \sigma_z^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{w})^2 = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} $$ **Step 3: Constrained optimization (1.5 marks)** Maximize $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}$ subject to $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} = 1$ using Lagrangian: $$ \mathcal{L} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} - \lambda (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{w} - 1) $$ Setting $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = 0$ gives $\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w} = \lambda \mathbf{w}$. (1.5 marks) **Step 4: Conclusion (1.5 marks)** The direction maximizing variance is the eigenvector $\mathbf{w}_1$ corresponding to the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$ of $\mathbf{\Sigma}$. By the **spectral theorem** for symmetric $\mathbf{\Sigma}$, all eigenvectors are orthogonal, so PC1, PC2, …, PCd are mutually uncorrelated. The $j^{th}$ PC's variance is $\lambda_j$. --- **Q3. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** *Given the centered dataset $\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \\ -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, compute the principal components, the explained variance ratio for PC1, and project the data onto PC1.* **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Covariance matrix (2 marks)** $$ \mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{4-1} \mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{X} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 & -2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & -2 \\ -2 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ \mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{3} \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 0 \\ 0 & 8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8/3 & 0 \\ 0 & 8/3 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2: Eigendecomposition (2 marks)** Since $\mathbf{\Sigma}$ is diagonal, eigenvalues are $\lambda_1 = \lambda_2 = 8/3 \approx 2.667$ with eigenvectors $\mathbf{w}_1 = (1,0)^T$ and $\mathbf{w}_2 = (0,1)^T$. **Step 3: Explained variance ratio (1.5 marks)** $$ \text{EVR}_1 = \frac{8/3}{8/3 + 8/3} = 0.5 = 50\% $$ **Step 4: Projection onto PC1 (1.5 marks)** $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{X} \mathbf{w}_1 = (2, 0, -2, 0)^T$. The 1D projection is $\mathbf{Z} = (2, 0, -2, 0)$, capturing **50% of the total variance** in a 1D representation (a degenerate case where the data is equally spread in both axes). --- ### Question B — Internal Choice Option 2 (14 Marks) **Q4. (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Explain the complete step-by-step PCA algorithm with a neat flow diagram. Discuss the role of mean-centering and standardization.* **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Input & pre-processing (2 marks)** Given $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$, **mean-center** each column: $$ \tilde{x}_{ij} = x_{ij} - \bar{x}_j, \quad \bar{x}_j = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} $$ If features have different units/scales, **standardize** to unit variance: $\tilde{x}_{ij} \leftarrow \tilde{x}_{ij} / s_j$ where $s_j$ is the standard deviation of feature $j$. Mean-centering ensures the principal axes pass through the centroid; standardization prevents high-variance features from dominating PC1. **Step 2: Covariance matrix (1.5 marks)** $$ \mathbf{\Sigma} = \frac{1}{n-1} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}} $$ **Step 3: Eigendecomposition and sorting (1.5 marks)** Compute eigenpairs $(\lambda_j, \mathbf{w}_j)$ and sort eigenvalues in descending order. The total variance is $\sum \lambda_j$. **Step 4: Selection of k and projection (2 marks)** Choose $k$ using CEVR threshold (typically 0.95). Form $\mathbf{W}_k$ and compute the reduced representation: $$ \mathbf{Z} = \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{W}_k \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times k} $$ **Flow Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1)**: Show the flowchart from raw data → centering → covariance → eigendecomposition → sorting → projection. --- **Q4. (b) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Discuss three methods for selecting the optimal number of principal components $k$. For a dataset where the eigenvalues are $\lambda = (4.5, 3.0, 1.8, 0.5, 0.2)$, determine the optimal $k$ using a 90% cumulative variance threshold.* **Step-by-step Model Solution:** **Step 1: Three methods (3 marks)** 1. **Cumulative Explained Variance (CEV) Threshold** — Pick the smallest $k$ with $\text{CEV}(k) \ge \text{threshold}$ (e.g., 0.90). 2. **Scree Plot / Cattell's Criterion** — Plot $\lambda_j$ vs. $j$ and pick the "elbow". 3. **Kaiser Criterion** — Retain all PCs with $\lambda_j \ge 1$ (for standardized data). **Step 2: Compute total variance (1 mark)** $$ V_{\text{total}} = 4.5 + 3.0 + 1.8 + 0.5 + 0.2 = 10.0 $$ **Step 3: Compute cumulative variance ratios (1.5 marks)** - $k=1$: $4.5 / 10.0 = 0.45$ - $k=2$: $7.5 / 10.0 = 0.75$ - $k=3$: $9.3 / 10.0 = 0.93$ ✓ (exceeds 0.90) - $k=4$: $9.8 / 10.0 = 0.98$ **Step 4: Conclusion (1.5 marks)** The smallest $k$ such that CEVR $\ge 0.90$ is $\boxed{k = 3}$, retaining 93% of the variance. Selecting $k=2$ (75%) is insufficient, and $k=3$ gives a substantial jump while avoiding the marginal component $\lambda_4 = 0.5$. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PCA (Module 2)** > > - **Definition:** Unsupervised, linear, orthogonal dimensionality-reduction technique that projects data onto directions of maximum variance. > - **Assumptions:** Linearity, second-order statistics sufficient, variance = information, orthogonal axes. > - **Algorithm:** Mean-center → Covariance matrix → Eigendecomposition → Sort → Select top $k$ → Project. > - **Key Identity:** $\mathbf{\Sigma} \mathbf{w}_j = \lambda_j \mathbf{w}_j$ — the direction of maximum variance is an eigenvector; the variance is the eigenvalue. > - **Explained Variance Ratio:** $\text{EVR}_j = \lambda_j / \sum_m \lambda_m$. > - **Reconstruction Error:** $J = \sum_{j=k+1}^{d} \lambda_j$ — minimised iff retained variance is maximised. > - **Selection of $k$:** CEVR threshold (0.90/0.95), scree-plot elbow, Kaiser rule ($\lambda_j \ge 1$). > - **PCA ≠ Feature Selection:** PCA **creates** new features (linear combinations); it does not subset the original ones. > - **Always mean-center** before covariance computation (or PC1 will be biased by the absolute mean). > - **Standardize** when features have very different units or scales. > - **Limitation:** PCA is **linear**; for non-linear manifolds, Kernel PCA / t-SNE / UMAP are needed. > - **Geometric Intuition:** Rotates the coordinate axes to align with directions of maximum spread. > - **Real-world Applications:** Face recognition (Eigenfaces), image compression, gene expression analysis, financial risk factor modelling, spectral data analysis. ---

topics.title: Clustering Algorithms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET527 - INTRODUCTION TO MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Clustering Algorithms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Clustering** is an **unsupervised learning** technique that partitions an unlabeled dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ into $K$ groups (clusters) $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$ such that data points within the same cluster are highly **similar (intra-cluster similarity)** and points across different clusters are highly **dissimilar (inter-cluster dissimilarity)**.

Mathematically, a clustering algorithm seeks to minimize an **objective function** $J$ that quantifies the compactness of the resulting partition. The most commonly used objective is the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**, also called **distortion** or **within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS)**:

$$J(C) = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^{2}$$

where $\mu_k$ is the **centroid** (mean vector) of cluster $C_k$.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (The "Library Sorting" Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine walking into a massive library where **10,000 books are scattered randomly on the floor** with no labels, no genres, and no shelves. You are asked to organize them. You naturally start forming piles: "These books have similar covers" (Science), "These look like novels," "These are thick dictionaries." You are **not told** the categories in advance — you discover them purely by measuring similarity between the books.
>
> This is exactly what **Clustering Algorithms** do with data. They discover hidden groupings based purely on intrinsic similarity, without any ground-truth labels.

### 1.3 Taxonomy of Clustering Algorithms

Clustering algorithms are broadly classified into several families, each based on a different mathematical principle:

| Family | Core Principle | Representative Algorithm |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Partition-based** | Divide data into $K$ non-overlapping clusters by minimizing intra-cluster variance | **K-Means**, K-Medoids |
| **Hierarchical** | Build a tree of clusters (dendrogram) by successive merging or splitting | **Agglomerative**, Divisive |
| **Density-based** | Group together points packed in dense regions; mark sparse points as noise | **DBSCAN**, OPTICS |
| **Model-based** | Assume data is generated from a mixture of probability distributions | **Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM)** |
| **Grid-based** | Quantize the space into a finite grid and perform clustering on the grid | STING, CLIQUE |

### 1.4 Why Clustering Matters in KTU Syllabus

> [!NOTE]
> Clustering is a **high-weightage topic in Module 2** of the KTU 2024 syllabus. Students are expected to (a) explain the algorithmic steps of K-Means, Hierarchical, and DBSCAN, (b) execute the K-Means algorithm on a numerical dataset by hand, and (c) compare algorithms based on complexity and use cases.

---

### 1.5 Distance Metrics — The Foundation of "Similarity"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every clustering algorithm depends on a distance (or similarity) metric.** The choice of metric directly determines cluster shape and algorithm behaviour. The KTU syllabus mandates the following five metrics for any $d$-dimensional points $x = (x_1, \ldots, x_d)$ and $y = (y_1, \ldots, y_d)$.

| Metric | Formula | Use Case |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Euclidean ($L_2$)** | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Default for K-Means, spherical data |
| **Manhattan ($L_1$)** | $d(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{d}\lvert x_i - y_i \rvert$ | High-dimensional sparse data |
| **Minkowski ($L_p$)** | $d(x,y) = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{d}\lvert x_i - y_i \rvert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | Generalized family of $L_1$ and $L_2$ |
| **Cosine** | $\cos\theta = \frac{x \cdot y}{\lVert x \rVert \lVert y \rVert}$ | Text mining, document clustering |
| **Mahalanobis** | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{(x-y)^T S^{-1} (x-y)}$ | Correlated features, statistical analysis |

Here, $S$ is the **covariance matrix** of the dataset. When $S = I$ (identity), Mahalanobis distance reduces to Euclidean distance.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 K-Means Clustering — The Workhorse of Partition Clustering

> [!NOTE]
> **K-Means** (Lloyd's algorithm, 1957) is the **most frequently examined clustering algorithm** in KTU. It is an **iterative, non-deterministic, partitional** method that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS).

#### 2.1.1 Algorithmic Intuition

The K-Means algorithm embodies a beautifully simple intuition:

1. **Place** $K$ initial "centroids" randomly in the feature space.
2. **Assign** every data point to its **nearest centroid** (E-step intuition).
3. **Recompute** each centroid as the **mean** of all points assigned to it (M-step intuition).
4. **Repeat** steps 2 and 3 until centroids stop moving (convergence).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> K-Means alternates between two sub-problems that are **jointly convex** but only **solved optimally one-at-a-time** when the other is fixed. This is the same Expectation–Maximization (EM) principle used in Gaussian Mixture Models.

#### 2.1.2 Formal Algorithm (Lloyd's Iteration)

**Input:** Dataset $X = \{x_1, \ldots, x_n\}$, number of clusters $K$, distance metric $d(\cdot,\cdot)$.

**Output:** Cluster assignments $C_1, \ldots, C_K$ and centroids $\mu_1, \ldots, \mu_K$.

**Step 1 — Initialization.** Choose $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$. Methods include:
- **Forgy:** Randomly select $K$ data points.
- **Random Partition:** Assign each point randomly to one of $K$ clusters, then take the mean.
- **K-Means++:** Probabilistically choose initial centroids weighted by squared distance from existing centroids (preferred — guarantees $O(\log K)$-competitive solution).

**Step 2 — Assignment (E-step).** For each $x_i$, assign it to the nearest centroid:

$$C_k^{(t)} = \left\{ x_i : d(x_i, \mu_k^{(t)}) \leq d(x_i, \mu_j^{(t)}) \; \forall j = 1, \ldots, K \right\}$$

**Step 3 — Update (M-step).** Recompute each centroid as the mean of its assigned points:

$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k^{(t)}} x_i$$

**Step 4 — Convergence Check.** If $\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \mu_k^{(t)}$ for all $k$, **stop**. Else, return to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the mean minimizes SSE?** Taking the partial derivative of $J = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$ with respect to $\mu_k$ and setting it to zero yields $\mu_k^* = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum x_i$. This is a **necessary condition for optimality** and is the rigorous justification for the M-step.

---

### 2.2 Hierarchical Clustering

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchical clustering** produces a nested tree of clusters called a **dendrogram**. Unlike K-Means, it does not require pre-specifying $K$ — the user can cut the dendrogram at any level to obtain the desired number of clusters.

#### 2.2.1 Two Variants

| Variant | Direction | Process |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Agglomerative (Bottom-Up)** | Start with $n$ singleton clusters, iteratively merge the **two closest** clusters | $O(n^3)$ naive, $O(n^2 \log n)$ with priority queue |
| **Divisive (Top-Down)** | Start with one cluster containing all points, iteratively split using a flat clustering algorithm (e.g., K-Means) | $O(2^n)$ naive |

Agglomerative is far more common in practice and in KTU examinations.

#### 2.2.2 Linkage Criteria — How to Measure Inter-Cluster Distance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> After each merge, the algorithm must decide which two clusters are "closest." The **linkage criterion** defines this distance $D(C_a, C_b)$:

$$\text{Single Linkage: } D(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x \in C_a, \, y \in C_b} d(x,y)$$

$$\text{Complete Linkage: } D(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x \in C_a, \, y \in C_b} d(x,y)$$

$$\text{Average Linkage: } D(C_a, C_b) = \frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \cdot \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{x \in C_a} \sum_{y \in C_b} d(x,y)$$

$$\text{Centroid Linkage: } D(C_a, C_b) = d(\mu_a, \mu_b)$$

$$\text{Ward's Linkage: } \Delta(A,B) = \frac{\vert A \vert \cdot \vert B \vert}{\vert A \vert + \vert B \vert} \cdot d(\mu_A, \mu_B)^2$$

| Linkage | Tendency | Sensitivity to Noise |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Single** | Forms long, chain-like clusters ("chaining effect") | High |
| **Complete** | Forms compact, equal-sized clusters | Low |
| **Average** | Compromise between single and complete | Medium |
| **Ward's** | Minimizes total within-cluster variance | Low |

---

### 2.3 DBSCAN — Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise

> [!NOTE]
> **DBSCAN** (Ester et al., 1996) defines clusters as **dense regions separated by low-density regions**. Crucially, it can discover **arbitrarily shaped clusters** and **identify noise/outliers** — capabilities that K-Means lacks.

#### 2.3.1 Two Hyperparameters

- $\varepsilon$ (**epsilon**): Radius of the neighbourhood.
- $\text{MinPts}$: Minimum number of points required to form a dense region.

#### 2.3.2 Three Point Classifications

For a point $p$:
- **Core Point:** $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$ (has at least MinPts neighbours within $\varepsilon$).
- **Border Point:** $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert < \text{MinPts}$ BUT lies within the $\varepsilon$-neighbourhood of a core point.
- **Noise Point (Outlier):** Neither a core nor a border point.

#### 2.3.3 Density Reachability and Connectivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A point $q$ is **directly density-reachable** from $p$ if $q \in N_\varepsilon(p)$ and $p$ is a core point. A point $q$ is **density-reachable** from $p$ if there exists a chain of points $p = p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n = q$ such that each $p_{i+1}$ is directly density-reachable from $p_i$. Two points are **density-connected** if there exists a point $o$ from which both are density-reachable. A **cluster** is a maximal set of density-connected points.

#### 2.3.4 Algorithm Steps

1. For each unvisited point $p$, mark it visited.
2. Find $N_\varepsilon(p)$. If $\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert \geq \text{MinPts}$, start a new cluster; else label $p$ as noise (temporarily).
3. Add all density-reachable points from $p$ to the cluster.
4. Repeat until all points are visited.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every equation you are expected to know for KTU 2024 Module 2 examinations.

| Concept | Formula | Symbols / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **K-Means Objective (WCSS)** | $J = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$ | $J$ is in units of squared distance |
| **Centroid Update** | $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ | $\vert C_k \vert$ = cluster size |
| **Euclidean Distance** | $d(x,y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{d}(x_i - y_i)^2}$ | $d$ = feature dimensionality |
| **Manhattan Distance** | $d(x,y) = \sum_{i=1}^{d}\lvert x_i - y_i \rvert$ | – |
| **Minkowski Distance** | $d_p(x,y) = \left(\sum \lvert x_i - y_i \rvert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | $p \geq 1$ |
| **Cosine Similarity** | $\cos\theta = \frac{\sum x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum x_i^2} \sqrt{\sum y_i^2}}$ | Range: $[-1, 1]$ |
| **Mahalanobis Distance** | $d_M = \sqrt{(x-y)^T S^{-1} (x-y)}$ | $S$ = covariance matrix |
| **Silhouette Coefficient** | $s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$ | $a(i)$ = mean intra-cluster distance, $b(i)$ = mean nearest-cluster distance; range $[-1, 1]$ |
| **DBSCAN Density** | Density at $p$ = $\frac{\vert N_\varepsilon(p) \vert}{\pi \varepsilon^2}$ (2-D) | – |
| **K-Means Time Complexity** | $O(n \cdot K \cdot I \cdot d)$ | $n$ = points, $K$ = clusters, $I$ = iterations, $d$ = dimensions |
| **Agglomerative HC Complexity** | $O(n^3)$ naive, $O(n^2 \log n)$ optimized | $n$ = number of points |
| **DBSCAN Complexity** | $O(n \log n)$ with spatial index, $O(n^2)$ naive | – |

---

### 2.5 Choosing the Optimal $K$ — The Elbow Method

> [!NOTE]
> Since K-Means requires pre-specifying $K$, KTU examiners frequently ask how to choose it. The **Elbow Method** plots $J(K)$ (WCSS) versus $K$ and selects the "elbow" point where the rate of decrease sharply slows.

The pseudo-code logic:

$$\text{Compute } J(K) \text{ for } K = 1, 2, \ldots, K_{\max}; \quad \text{Choose } K^* = \arg\min_K \left\{ J(K) - \alpha \cdot K \right\}$$

where $\alpha$ is a regularization constant (alternative: use the **Silhouette score** to identify the $K$ maximizing average $s(i)$).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Centroid Update Formula Minimizes WCSS

> [!NOTE]
> We will rigorously prove that the centroid of a cluster that minimizes the within-cluster sum of squared Euclidean distances is the **arithmetic mean** of the points in that cluster. This is a KTU favourite.

**Statement.** Given a cluster $C_k$ containing $n_k$ points, the value of $\mu_k$ that minimizes $J_k = \sum_{x_i \in C_k} \lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2$ is $\mu_k^* = \frac{1}{n_k}\sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$.

**Proof.** Expand the squared norm and use the identity $\lVert x_i - \mu_k \rVert^2 = (x_i - \mu_k)^T (x_i - \mu_k)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_k(\mu_k) &= \sum_{i=1}^{n_k} (x_i - \mu_k)^T (x_i - \mu_k) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n_k} \left( x_i^T x_i - 2 x_i^T \mu_k + \mu_k^T \mu_k \right) \\
&= \sum_{i=1}^{n_k} x_i^T x_i \;-\; 2 \mu_k^T \sum_{i=1}^{n_k} x_i \;+\; n_k \cdot \mu_k^T \mu_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Take the gradient** of $J_k$ with respect to the vector $\mu_k$. The first term is constant (does not depend on $\mu_k$). The derivative of $-2\mu_k^T \sum x_i$ is $-2\sum x_i$. The derivative of $n_k \cdot \mu_k^T \mu_k$ is $2 n_k \mu_k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mu_k} J_k &= -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n_k} x_i + 2 n_k \mu_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Set the gradient to zero** to find the critical point. This is the standard first-order necessary condition for an unconstrained minimum (the function is convex, so the critical point is the global minimum).

$$
\begin{aligned}
-2 \sum_{i=1}^{n_k} x_i + 2 n_k \mu_k &= 0 \\
2 n_k \mu_k &= 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n_k} x_i \\
\mu_k^* &= \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{i=1}^{n_k} x_i
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Confirm convexity** by checking the Hessian matrix. The second derivative (Hessian) of $J_k$ is $2 n_k I$ (where $I$ is the identity matrix), which is **positive definite** for $n_k \geq 1$. Therefore $J_k$ is strictly convex, confirming that $\mu_k^*$ is the **unique global minimum**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example: K-Means on a 2-D Dataset

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU frequently tests hand-computation of K-Means.** The following is a complete, step-by-step execution.

**Dataset (6 points in $\mathbb{R}^2$):**
$$X = \{A(2,10),\; B(2,5),\; C(8,4),\; D(5,8),\; E(7,5),\; F(6,4)\}$$

**Task:** Run K-Means with $K = 2$ and initial centroids $\mu_1^{(0)} = (2,10)$ (point A) and $\mu_2^{(0)} = (5,8)$ (point D). Use **Euclidean distance**.

#### ITERATION 1 — Assignment Step

Compute Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids. Assign each point to the closer centroid.

| Point | Coordinates | $d(\cdot, \mu_1)$ | $d(\cdot, \mu_2)$ | Assigned Cluster |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **A** | (2, 10) | $\sqrt{0^2 + 0^2} = 0.00$ | $\sqrt{3^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{13} = 3.61$ | **C₁** |
| **B** | (2, 5) | $\sqrt{0^2 + 5^2} = 5.00$ | $\sqrt{3^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{18} = 4.24$ | **C₂** |
| **C** | (8, 4) | $\sqrt{6^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{72} = 8.49$ | $\sqrt{3^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00$ | **C₂** |
| **D** | (5, 8) | $\sqrt{3^2 + 2^2} = 3.61$ | $\sqrt{0^2 + 0^2} = 0.00$ | **C₂** |
| **E** | (7, 5) | $\sqrt{5^2 + 5^2} = \sqrt{50} = 7.07$ | $\sqrt{2^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{13} = 3.61$ | **C₂** |
| **F** | (6, 4) | $\sqrt{4^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{52} = 7.21$ | $\sqrt{1^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{17} = 4.12$ | **C₂** |

**Result of Iteration 1 Assignment:**
$$C_1^{(1)} = \{A(2,10)\}, \quad C_2^{(1)} = \{B(2,5),\; C(8,4),\; D(5,8),\; E(7,5),\; F(6,4)\}$$

#### ITERATION 1 — Update Step

Recompute centroids as the **arithmetic mean** of assigned points.

For $C_1^{(1)} = \{A(2,10)\}$ — only one point, so the centroid remains unchanged:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_1^{(1)} &= \frac{(2,10)}{1} = (2,\; 10)
\end{aligned}
$$

For $C_2^{(1)} = \{B, C, D, E, F\}$ — sum coordinates and divide by 5:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_2^{(1)} &= \left( \frac{2+8+5+7+6}{5},\; \frac{5+4+8+5+4}{5} \right) \\
&= \left( \frac{28}{5},\; \frac{26}{5} \right) \\
&= (5.6,\; 5.2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Centroids after Iteration 1:** $\mu_1^{(1)} = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2^{(1)} = (5.6, 5.2)$. **Centroids have changed** ($\mu_2$ moved from $(5,8)$ to $(5.6, 5.2)$), so we must iterate again.

#### ITERATION 2 — Assignment Step

Recompute distances to the new centroids.

| Point | Coordinates | $d(\cdot, \mu_1^{(1)})$ | $d(\cdot, \mu_2^{(1)})$ | Assigned Cluster |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **A** | (2, 10) | $0.00$ | $\sqrt{3.6^2 + 4.8^2} = \sqrt{36} = 6.00$ | **C₁** |
| **B** | (2, 5) | $5.00$ | $\sqrt{3.6^2 + 0.2^2} = \sqrt{13} = 3.61$ | **C₂** |
| **C** | (8, 4) | $8.49$ | $\sqrt{2.4^2 + 1.2^2} = \sqrt{7.2} = 2.68$ | **C₂** |
| **D** | (5, 8) | $3.61$ | $\sqrt{0.6^2 + 2.8^2} = \sqrt{8.2} = 2.86$ | **C₂** |
| **E** | (7, 5) | $7.07$ | $\sqrt{1.4^2 + 0.2^2} = \sqrt{2.0} = 1.41$ | **C₂** |
| **F** | (6, 4) | $7.21$ | $\sqrt{0.4^2 + 1.2^2} = \sqrt{1.6} = 1.26$ | **C₂** |

**Result of Iteration 2 Assignment:**
$$C_1^{(2)} = \{A\}, \quad C_2^{(2)} = \{B, C, D, E, F\}$$

**Assignments are unchanged from Iteration 1.**

#### ITERATION 2 — Update Step

Recompute $\mu_2$ with the same five points:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_2^{(2)} &= \left( \frac{2+8+5+7+6}{5},\; \frac{5+4+8+5+4}{5} \right) = (5.6,\; 5.2)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Centroids after Iteration 2:** $\mu_1^{(2)} = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2^{(2)} = (5.6, 5.2)$. These **match** the centroids from Iteration 1.

#### Convergence Achieved ✓

Since $\mu_1^{(2)} = \mu_1^{(1)}$ and $\mu_2^{(2)} = \mu_2^{(1)}$, the algorithm has **converged**.

#### Final WCSS Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{\text{final}} &= \sum_{x_i \in C_1} \lVert x_i - \mu_1 \rVert^2 + \sum_{x_i \in C_2} \lVert x_i - \mu_2 \rVert^2 \\
&= \underbrace{0}_{A \to \mu_1} + \underbrace{13.00}_{B} + \underbrace{7.20}_{C} + \underbrace{8.20}_{D} + \underbrace{2.00}_{E} + \underbrace{1.60}_{F} \\
&= 0 + 13.00 + 7.20 + 8.20 + 2.00 + 1.60 \\
&= 32.00
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Clusters:** $C_1 = \{(2,10)\}$, $C_2 = \{(2,5), (8,4), (5,8), (7,5), (6,4)\}$ with centroids $(2, 10)$ and $(5.6, 5.2)$ and WCSS $= 32.0$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Silhouette Coefficient for Cluster Validation

> [!NOTE]
> The **Silhouette score** is a KTU-favoured internal validation metric. It measures how well each point lies within its cluster.

For a point $x_i \in C_k$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a(i) &= \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert - 1} \sum_{x_j \in C_k,\, j \neq i} d(x_i, x_j) \\
b(i) &= \min_{C_l \neq C_k} \left\{ \frac{1}{\vert C_l \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_l} d(x_i, x_j) \right\} \\
s(i) &= \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i),\; b(i)\}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation table:**

| Value of $s(i)$ | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| $s(i) \approx +1$ | Point is well-clustered (far from neighbouring clusters) |
| $s(i) \approx 0$ | Point lies on the boundary between two clusters |
| $s(i) \approx -1$ | Point is likely misclassified (closer to another cluster) |

The **average silhouette score** $\bar{S} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} s(i)$ is used to globally evaluate clustering quality, with $\bar{S} \to 1$ indicating strong cluster structure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
```

```mermaid
    B2 --> B3[IDENTIFY TWO CLOSEST CLUSTERS USING LINKAGE CRITERION]
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C3 --> C8[NO] --> C9{HAS A CORE POINT NEIGHBOR}

    C11 --> C7
    C13 --> C7
    C7 --> C14{ALL POINTS VISITED}
    C14 --> C15[NO] --> C1
    C14 --> C16[YES] --> C17[END: OUTPUT CLUSTERS AND NOISE LABELS]
```
