KTU frequently tests: > - Computing **Entropy**, **Gini**, and **Information Gain** on toy datasets. > - The **ID3** vs **CART** algorithm differences. > - **Pruning** strategies to avoid overfitting. > - Continuous attribute handling via **threshold-based binary splitting**. ## 1.4 Types of Decision Tree Algorithms | Algorithm | Splitting Criterion | Branching | Task | Developer | |---|---|---|---|---| | **ID3** | Information Gain | Multi-way | Classification | Quinlan (1986) | | **C4.5** | Gain Ratio | Multi-way | Classification | Quinlan (1993) | | **CART** | Gini / Variance Reduction | Binary | Classification & Regression | Breiman (1984) | | **CHAID** | Chi-Square | Multi-way | Classification | Kass (1980) | ## 1.5 Geometric Intuition — Axis-Parallel Partitions A Decision Tree divides the input space using **axis-aligned (axis-parallel) decision boundaries**. Each split is parallel to one of the feature axes, producing **rectangular (hyper-rectangular) decision regions**. This makes DTs: - **Interpretable** — boundaries are simple thresholds like $X_1 \leq 5.2$. - **Limited** — they cannot natively capture diagonal or curved boundaries. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — How a Decision Tree is Built The Tree Induction process follows a **Greedy, Top-Down, Recursive Partitioning** strategy (also called **TDIDT** — Top-Down Induction of Decision Trees). > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Core Loop (TDIDT Algorithm):** > 1. Start at the **root** with the full training set $S$. > 2. Examine **all** features and **all** possible split points. > 3. Select the (feature, split-point) pair that **maximizes a "purity" metric** (Information Gain, Gain Ratio, or Gini decrease). > 4. **Partition** $S$ into child subsets. > 5. **Recurse** on each child subset. > 6. **Stop** when a stopping condition is met (pure node, max depth, min samples, etc.). > 7. Apply **pruning** to combat overfitting. This is a **Hunt's Algorithm** (1966) variant — the conceptual ancestor of all modern DT algorithms. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All exam-critical formulas for Decision Trees — memorize these.** | # | Metric | Formula | Range | Used By | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | ID3, C4.5 | | 2 | **Conditional Entropy** | $H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | ID3, C4.5 | | 3 | **Information Gain** | $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | $[0, H(S)]$ | ID3 | | 4 | **Split Information (Intrinsic Value)** | $SI(S, A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | $[0, \log_2 v]$ | C4.5 | | 5 | **Gain Ratio** | $GR(S, A) = \dfrac{IG(S, A)}{SI(S, A)}$ | $[0, 1]$ | C4.5 | | 6 | **Gini Index** | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^{2}$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | CART | | 7 | **Gini Gain (Impurity Decrease)** | $\Delta Gini(S, A) = Gini(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_v)$ | $[0, 0.5]$ | CART | | 8 | **Classification Error** | $E(S) = 1 - \max_i p_i$ | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | Alternative | | 9 | **Regression Variance Reduction** | $\Delta Var = Var(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Var(S_v)$ | $[0, Var(S)]$ | CART Regression | | 10 | **Predicted Class at Leaf** | $\hat{y} = \underset{c}{\arg\max}\, p_c$ | — | Classification | ### Boundary & Edge Conditions - $0 \log 0 \equiv 0$ (by convention, since $\lim_{x \to 0^+} x \log x = 0$). - $H(S) = 0 \iff$ node is **pure** (all samples belong to one class). - $Gini(S) = 0 \iff$ node is **pure**. - $IG(S, A) = 0 \iff$ attribute $A$ provides **no information** about the class. - If $SI(S, A) = 0$, Gain Ratio is **undefined** (C4.5 handles this by ignoring the attribute). ### Why These Formulas? (Engineering Utility) - **Entropy** comes from **Information Theory** (Shannon, 1948) — measures expected number of bits needed to encode class labels. - **Gini** comes from **economics** (Gini coefficient, 1912) — measures misclassification cost if we randomly label a sample according to class distribution. - **Gain Ratio** corrects ID3's **multi-branch bias** (it penalizes attributes with many values, e.g., "Date" or "ID"). - These formulas are used in **bioinformatics, medical diagnosis, credit scoring, customer churn prediction, and remote sensing** classification. ## 2.3 Continuous Attribute Handling For a continuous attribute $A$, sort values and evaluate candidate split thresholds at every midpoint between consecutive distinct values: $$T_{candidate} = \frac{a_i + a_{i+1}}{2}$$ Select the threshold that maximizes the chosen metric. This converts a continuous attribute into a **binary test**: $A \leq t$ vs $A > t$. > [!TIP] > **Computational Trick**: For $n$ sorted values, there are at most $n - 1$ candidate thresholds — never evaluate all $\mathbb{R}$. ## 2.4 Overfitting and Pruning > [!WARNING] > **Overfitting is the #1 DT pitfall** — fully grown trees perfectly memorize training data but fail on unseen data. **Pruning Strategies:** | Strategy | When | Methods | Pros | Cons | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping)** | During growth | Set `max_depth`, `min_samples_split`, `min_samples_leaf`, `max_leaf_nodes` | Fast, prevents over-specialization | May stop too early (underfitting) | | **Post-Pruning (Reduced-Error / Cost-Complexity)** | After full growth | Replace subtrees with leaves using validation set or $\alpha$-penalty | More accurate trees | Computationally expensive | **Cost-Complexity Pruning (CART's CCP — $\alpha$-pruning):** $$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \cdot \vert \text{leaves}(T) \vert$$ where $R(T)$ is the misclassification rate and $\alpha \geq 0$ is a complexity parameter. As $\alpha$ increases, the tree is pruned more aggressively. ## 2.5 Handling Missing Values - **Surrogate splits** (CART): use a correlated alternative feature when the primary feature value is missing. - **Probability splits**: distribute the sample fractionally across all branches proportional to branch sizes. - **Imputation**: fill missing values with mean/median (continuous) or mode (categorical) before training. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Example ## 3.1 Canonical Example — The "Play Tennis" Dataset (Adapted for KTU) Consider the following training set $S$ with 10 samples, 4 features, and binary class $C = \{Yes, No\}$: | Day | Outlook ($X_1$) | Temperature ($X_2$) | Humidity ($X_3$) | Wind ($X_4$) | Play ($C$) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | **No** | | 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | **No** | | 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | **Yes** | | 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | **Yes** | | 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | **Yes** | | 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | **No** | | 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | **Yes** | | 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | **No** | | 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | **Yes** | | 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | **Yes** | Class distribution: **Yes = 6, No = 4** → $p_{Yes} = 0.6$, $p_{No} = 0.4$. --- ### STEP 1 — Compute Entropy of the Root Node $H(S)$ $$H(S) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$$ $$H(S) = -[p_{Yes} \log_2 p_{Yes} + p_{No} \log_2 p_{No}]$$ $$H(S) = -\left[0.6 \log_2 0.6 + 0.4 \log_2 0.4\right]$$ Computing the logarithms: - $\log_2 0.6 = \log_2(3/5) \approx -0.7370$ - $\log_2 0.4 = \log_2(2/5) \approx -1.3219$ $$H(S) = -[(0.6)(-0.7370) + (0.4)(-1.3219)]$$ $$H(S) = -[-0.4422 + (-0.5288)] = -[-0.9710]$$ $$\boxed{H(S) \approx 0.971 \text{ bits}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **total disorder** at the root. Our goal is to choose a feature that reduces this disorder the most. --- ### STEP 2 — Compute Information Gain for each Feature #### 2(a) — $IG(S, \text{Outlook})$ **Outlook** has 3 values: Sunny (3 samples), Overcast (2 samples), Rain (5 samples). **Subset $S_{Sunny}$** (Days 1, 2, 8): Yes=1, No=2 → $p_{Yes}=1/3$, $p_{No}=2/3$ $$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\left[\frac{1}{3}\log_2\frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3}\log_2\frac{2}{3}\right]$$ $$H(S_{Sunny}) = -\left[\frac{1}{3}(-1.5850) + \frac{2}{3}(-0.5850)\right] = -[-0.5283 - 0.3900] = 0.9183$$ **Subset $S_{Overcast}$** (Days 3, 7): Yes=2, No=0 → **Pure node**! $$H(S_{Overcast}) = -[1 \log_2 1 + 0] = 0$$ **Subset $S_{Rain}$** (Days 4, 5, 6, 9, 10): Yes=4, No=1 → $p_{Yes}=4/5$, $p_{No}=1/5$ $$H(S_{Rain}) = -\left[\frac{4}{5}\log_2\frac{4}{5} + \frac{1}{5}\log_2\frac{1}{5}\right]$$ $$H(S_{Rain}) = -\left[0.8(-0.3219) + 0.2(-2.3219)\right] = -[-0.2575 - 0.4644] = 0.7219$$ **Conditional Entropy:** $$H(S \mid Outlook) = \frac{3}{10}(0.9183) + \frac{2}{10}(0) + \frac{5}{10}(0.7219)$$ $$H(S \mid Outlook) = 0.2755 + 0 + 0.3610 = 0.6365$$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, Outlook) = H(S) - H(S \mid Outlook) = 0.971 - 0.6365$$ $$\boxed{IG(S, Outlook) \approx 0.335 \text{ bits}}$$ --- #### 2(b) — $IG(S, \text{Humidity})$ **Humidity** has 2 values: High (5 samples — Days 1,2,3,4,8), Normal (5 samples — Days 5,6,7,9,10). **Subset $S_{High}$**: Yes=2 (Days 3,4), No=3 (Days 1,2,8) → $p_{Yes}=2/5, p_{No}=3/5$ $$H(S_{High}) = -\left[\frac{2}{5}\log_2\frac{2}{5} + \frac{3}{5}\log_2\frac{3}{5}\right] = -[0.4(-1.3219) + 0.6(-0.7370)]$$ $$H(S_{High}) = -[-0.5288 - 0.4422] = 0.9710$$ **Subset $S_{Normal}$**: Yes=4 (Days 5,7,9,10), No=1 (Day 6) → $p_{Yes}=4/5, p_{No}=1/5$ $$H(S_{Normal}) = -\left[\frac{4}{5}\log_2\frac{4}{5} + \frac{1}{5}\log_2\frac{1}{5}\right] = 0.7219 \text{ (computed above)}$$ **Conditional Entropy:** $$H(S \mid Humidity) = \frac{5}{10}(0.9710) + \frac{5}{10}(0.7219) = 0.4855 + 0.3610 = 0.8465$$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, Humidity) = 0.971 - 0.8465$$ $$\boxed{IG(S, Humidity) \approx 0.125 \text{ bits}}$$ --- #### 2(c) — $IG(S, \text{Wind})$ **Wind** has 2 values: Weak (6 samples — Days 1,3,4,5,8,9,10 — wait, let me recount). Recounting: - **Weak**: Days 1, 3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10 → 7 samples (Yes: 3,4,5,9,10 = 5 Yes; No: 1,8 = 2 No) — let me recheck from the table. | Day | Wind | Play | |---|---|---| | 1 | Weak | No | | 2 | Strong | No | | 3 | Weak | Yes | | 4 | Weak | Yes | | 5 | Weak | Yes | | 6 | Strong | No | | 7 | Strong | Yes | | 8 | Weak | No | | 9 | Weak | Yes | | 10 | Weak | Yes | **Weak** (7 samples: Days 1,3,4,5,8,9,10): Yes=5, No=2 → $p_{Yes}=5/7, p_{No}=2/7$ $$H(S_{Weak}) = -\left[\frac{5}{7}\log_2\frac{5}{7} + \frac{2}{7}\log_2\frac{2}{7}\right]$$ $$\frac{5}{7} \approx 0.7143, \log_2(0.7143) \approx -0.4854$$ $$\frac{2}{7} \approx 0.2857, \log_2(0.2857) \approx -1.8074$$ $$H(S_{Weak}) = -[(0.7143)(-0.4854) + (0.2857)(-1.8074)] = -[-0.3467 - 0.5164] = 0.8631$$ **Strong** (3 samples: Days 2,6,7): Yes=1, No=2 → $p_{Yes}=1/3, p_{No}=2/3$ $$H(S_{Strong}) = 0.9183 \text{ (same as Sunny case)}$$ **Conditional Entropy:** $$H(S \mid Wind) = \frac{7}{10}(0.8631) + \frac{3}{10}(0.9183) = 0.6042 + 0.2755 = 0.8797$$ **Information Gain:** $$IG(S, Wind) = 0.971 - 0.8797$$ $$\boxed{IG(S, Wind) \approx 0.091 \text{ bits}}$$ --- ### STEP 3 — Decision at the Root | Feature | Information Gain | |---|---| | **Outlook** | **0.335** ← WINNER | | Humidity | 0.125 | | Wind | 0.091 | $$\boxed{\text{Root Split} = \arg\max_A IG(S, A) = \text{Outlook}}$$ The tree starts at the root with a test on **Outlook**. > [!TIP] > **Key observation**: Since $S_{Overcast}$ is **pure** (entropy = 0), it becomes a **leaf node** immediately with class = **Yes**. No further splitting needed there. --- ### STEP 4 — Recurse on $S_{Sunny}$ and $S_{Rain}$ For brevity, we now compute Information Gain on the remaining attributes for $S_{Sunny}$ (3 samples: 1 Yes, 2 No). The root entropy is: $$H(S_{Sunny}) = 0.9183 \text{ bits}$$ The process repeats for Temperature, Humidity, and Wind on this subset. The attribute with maximum IG is selected, and the recursion continues. This is exactly how ID3 builds the tree top-down. --- ## 3.2 Gini Index Worked Example (CART) Using the same root $S$ with $p_{Yes}=0.6, p_{No}=0.4$: $$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_i p_i^2 = 1 - (0.6^2 + 0.4^2) = 1 - (0.36 + 0.16) = 1 - 0.52$$ $$\boxed{Gini(S) = 0.48}$$ For $S_{Overcast}$ (pure: 2 Yes, 0 No, $p_{Yes}=1$): $$Gini(S_{Overcast}) = 1 - (1^2 + 0^2) = 0$$ **Gini Gain for Outlook:** $$\Delta Gini(S, Outlook) = 0.48 - \left[\frac{3}{10}Gini(S_{Sunny}) + \frac{2}{10}Gini(S_{Overcast}) + \frac{5}{10}Gini(S_{Rain})\right]$$ Where: - $Gini(S_{Sunny}) = 1 - ((1/3)^2 + (2/3)^2) = 1 - (0.111 + 0.444) = 0.444$ - $Gini(S_{Overcast}) = 0$ - $Gini(S_{Rain}) = 1 - ((4/5)^2 + (1/5)^2) = 1 - (0.64 + 0.04) = 0.32$ $$\Delta Gini(S, Outlook) = 0.48 - [0.3(0.444) + 0.2(0) + 0.5(0.32)] = 0.48 - [0.1333 + 0 + 0.16]$$ $$\boxed{\Delta Gini(S, Outlook) = 0.48 - 0.2933 = 0.1867}$$ ## 3.3 Regression Trees — Variance Reduction For regression, the splitting criterion is **Variance Reduction**: $$\Delta Var(S, A) = Var(S) - \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Var(S_v)$$ where $Var(S) = \frac{1}{\vert S \vert}\sum_{y \in S}(y - \bar{y})^2$. The leaf prediction is $\bar{y}$ (the mean of the target values in that leaf). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Decision Tree Architecture (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeS(["START: Training Set S"]):::root N1{"Test on Outlook?"}:::internal N2{"Test on Humidity?"}:::internal N3{"Test on Wind?"}:::internal N4{"Test on Temperature?"}:::internal L1(["Leaf: Play = Yes"]):::leaf L2(["Leaf: Play = No"]):::leaf L3(["Leaf: Play = Yes"]):::leaf L4(["Leaf: Play = No"]):::leaf L5(["Leaf: Play = Yes"]):::leaf L6(["Leaf: Play = No"]):::leaf nodeS --> N1 N1 -- Sunny --> N2 N1 -- Overcast --> L1 N1 -- Rain --> N3 N2 -- High --> L2 N2 -- Normal --> L3 N3 -- Weak --> L4 N3 -- Strong --> N5 N5["Test on Temperature?"]:::internal N5 -- Mild --> L5 N5 -- Cool --> L6 classDef root fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef internal fill:#4472c4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.2 Tree Induction Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR A(["Training Data S"]):::input --> B["Preprocessing
Handle Missing, Encode Categorical"]:::pre B --> C{"Stopping Criteria Met?"}:::check C -- No --> D["Evaluate All Features
All Split Points"]:::eval D --> E["Compute IG or Gini Gain
for Each Candidate"]:::metric E --> F["Select Best Split
argmax metric"]:::select F --> G["Partition S into
Subsets S_v"]:::partition G --> C C -- Yes --> H["Apply Pruning
Pre or Post"]:::prune H --> I(["Final Decision Tree"]):::output classDef input fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef pre fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000 classDef check fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000 classDef eval fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000 classDef metric fill:#a9d18e,stroke:#000 classDef select fill:#70ad47,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef partition fill:#9dc3e6,stroke:#000 classDef prune fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff classDef output fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` ## 4.3 ID3 vs CART — Algorithmic Comparison Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ID3["ID3 Algorithm"] I1["Uses Information Gain"] --> I2["Multi-way Splits"] --> I3["Classification Only"] --> I4["Biased Toward
Many-Valued Attributes"] end subgraph C45["C4.5 Algorithm"] C1["Uses Gain Ratio"] --> C2["Multi-way Splits"] --> C3["Handles Continuous
Attributes"] --> C4["Includes Pruning"] end subgraph CART["CART Algorithm"] R1["Uses Gini Index"] --> R2["Binary Splits Only"] --> R3["Classification + Regression"] --> R4["Cost-Complexity
Pruning"] end style ID3 fill:#deebf7,stroke:#2e75b6 style C45 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000 style CART fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#c55a11 ``` ## 4.4 Geometric Interpretation of Decision Boundaries ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph FEAT["2D Feature Space Partitioning"] P1["Split X1 less than 3"] --> P2["Top-Left: Class A"] P1 --> P3["Top-Right: Test X2 less than 5"] P3 --> P4["Class B"] P3 --> P5["Test X1 less than 7"] P5 --> P6["Class C"] P5 --> P7["Class D"] end P0["Input Space R2"]:::input --> P1 style FEAT fill:#f2f2f2,stroke:#595959 classDef input fill:#4472c4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation**: Each split carves a rectangular region from the prior one. The leaves of the tree form a **disjoint union** covering the entire input space, with each region assigned a single class label or regression value. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Set] **Define Information Gain. Explain its role in Decision Tree induction.** **Model Answer:** > **Information Gain (IG)** is the reduction in entropy (or impurity) of the target variable achieved by partitioning the dataset $S$ based on a feature $A$. $$IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$ where $H(S)$ is the entropy of the original set and $H(S \mid A)$ is the conditional entropy after the split. **Role in DT Induction**: - ID3 algorithm uses IG to select the **best splitting attribute** at each node. - The attribute with the **highest Information Gain** is chosen because it most effectively reduces uncertainty about the class label. - It quantifies how well a feature separates the training samples according to their target classification. **(3 marks: 1.5 for definition + formula, 1.5 for role)** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Set] **What is the Gini Index? How is it used in the CART algorithm?** **Model Answer:** > The **Gini Index** measures the impurity of a dataset $S$ with $c$ classes: $$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i^{2}$$ where $p_i$ is the probability of class $i$ in $S$. **Use in CART**: - CART uses the **Gini Gain** $\Delta Gini(S, A) = Gini(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} Gini(S_v)$ to choose the best feature. - The attribute with the **largest Gini decrease** is selected. - Gini is computationally faster than entropy (no logarithm) and is the **default criterion in scikit-learn's `DecisionTreeClassifier`**. **(3 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for interpretation, 1 for CART usage)** --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. The model solutions below are exhaustive and aligned with KTU valuation expectations. --- ### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Set] **Q. (a)** Given the following training dataset for the binary classification problem (class: $\pm$), construct a Decision Tree using the **ID3 algorithm** with **Information Gain** as the splitting criterion. Show **all** entropy and IG calculations at each node. | # | Color | Size | Shape | Class | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Red | Small | Circle | + | | 2 | Red | Large | Triangle | − | | 3 | Green | Small | Circle | + | | 4 | Green | Large | Circle | + | | 5 | Red | Small | Circle | + | | 6 | Green | Large | Triangle | − | | 7 | Red | Small | Triangle | − | | 8 | Green | Small | Circle | + | **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Root Entropy** Total: 8 samples, Class $+$ = 5, Class $-$ = 3. $p_+ = 5/8, p_- = 3/8$. $$H(S) = -\left[\frac{5}{8}\log_2\frac{5}{8} + \frac{3}{8}\log_2\frac{3}{8}\right]$$ $\log_2(5/8) = \log_2 0.625 \approx -0.6781$, $\log_2(3/8) = \log_2 0.375 \approx -1.4150$ $$H(S) = -[(0.625)(-0.6781) + (0.375)(-1.4150)] = -[-0.4238 - 0.5306] = 0.9544 \text{ bits}$$ **Step 2 — IG for "Color"** - $S_{Red}$ (samples 1, 2, 5, 7): $+$=3, $-$=1 → $p_+=3/4, p_-=1/4$ $$H(S_{Red}) = -\left[\frac{3}{4}\log_2\frac{3}{4} + \frac{1}{4}\log_2\frac{1}{4}\right] = -[0.75(-0.4150) + 0.25(-2)] = 0.8113$$ - $S_{Green}$ (samples 3, 4, 6, 8): $+$=3, $-$=1 → same as above $$H(S_{Green}) = 0.8113$$ $$H(S \mid Color) = \frac{4}{8}(0.8113) + \frac{4}{8}(0.8113) = 0.8113$$ $$IG(S, Color) = 0.9544 - 0.8113 = 0.1431$$ **Step 3 — IG for "Size"** - $S_{Small}$ (samples 1, 3, 5, 7, 8): $+$=4, $-$=1 $$H(S_{Small}) = -\left[\frac{4}{5}\log_2\frac{4}{5} + \frac{1}{5}\log_2\frac{1}{5}\right] = -[0.8(-0.3219) + 0.2(-2.3219)] = 0.7219$$ - $S_{Large}$ (samples 2, 4, 6): $+$=1, $-$=2 $$H(S_{Large}) = -\left[\frac{1}{3}\log_2\frac{1}{3} + \frac{2}{3}\log_2\frac{2}{3}\right] = 0.9183$$ $$H(S \mid Size) = \frac{5}{8}(0.7219) + \frac{3}{8}(0.9183) = 0.4512 + 0.3444 = 0.7956$$ $$IG(S, Size) = 0.9544 - 0.7956 = 0.1588$$ **Step 4 — IG for "Shape"** - $S_{Circle}$ (samples 1, 3, 4, 5, 8): $+$=5, $-$=0 → Pure! $$H(S_{Circle}) = 0$$ - $S_{Triangle}$ (samples 2, 6, 7): $+$=0, $-$=3 → Pure! $$H(S_{Triangle}) = 0$$ $$H(S \mid Shape) = \frac{5}{8}(0) + \frac{3}{8}(0) = 0$$ $$IG(S, Shape) = 0.9544 - 0 = \boxed{0.9544 \text{ bits (MAXIMUM)}}$$ **Step 5 — Root Decision** Shape has the highest IG. Split on **Shape**: - **Circle** branch → Leaf: Class $+$ (pure) - **Triangle** branch → Leaf: Class $-$ (pure) $$\boxed{\text{Resulting Tree: Root = Shape. Both branches are pure leaves.}}$$ **Tree structure (textual):** ``` [Shape?] / \ Circle Triangle | | Class + Class − ``` **(7 marks: 2 for setup/root entropy, 3 for IG of all three features, 1 for selection, 1 for final tree)** --- **Q. (b)** Compare **Gini Index** and **Entropy** as impurity measures. Discuss the impact of using Gain Ratio over plain Information Gain. **Model Answer:** **Comparison Table:** | Aspect | Gini Index | Entropy | |---|---|---| | **Formula** | $1 - \sum p_i^2$ | $-\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | | **Range** | $[0, 1 - 1/c]$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ | | **Computation** | No logarithm — faster | Requires $\log_2$ — slower | | **Sensitivity** | Less sensitive to class probability changes | More sensitive near $p = 0.5$ | | **Theoretical Basis** | Misclassification cost (CART) | Information theory (Shannon) | | **Tree Quality** | Often produces similar splits | Theoretically optimal | | **Used By** | CART (default in scikit-learn) | ID3, C4.5 | **Key Observations:** 1. Both Gini and Entropy are **monotonically related** (similar splits in most practical cases). 2. **Gini is computationally cheaper** — it involves only squaring, no logarithms. This is why scikit-learn defaults to it. 3. **Entropy has stronger mathematical justification** from information theory and is more sensitive to changes in node probability distributions. **Impact of Gain Ratio over Information Gain:** $$\text{Information Gain (IG)}: \text{Bias toward multi-valued attributes.}$$ For example, an attribute like "Date" or "Customer_ID" can have thousands of unique values. It might produce many pure leaves (one per value), giving **artificially high IG**. **Solution — Gain Ratio (GR):** $$GR(S, A) = \frac{IG(S, A)}{SI(S, A)}$$ where $SI(S, A) = -\sum_v \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ is the **Split Information** (intrinsic value). - $SI$ **penalizes** attributes that produce many small partitions. - $GR$ **normalizes** IG by the number of branches. - This is why **C4.5 uses Gain Ratio** — it corrects ID3's bias. **Trade-off**: Gain Ratio may over-correct, sometimes favoring attributes with very few branches. C4.5 handles this by computing GR only for attributes with **above-average IG** first. **(7 marks: 3 for comparison, 2 for Gini vs Entropy nuances, 2 for Gain Ratio correction mechanism)** --- ### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q. (a)** Explain the **CART algorithm** for building classification trees. How does it differ from ID3 in terms of split type, criterion, and output type? Compute the **Gini Index** for a node $S$ with 12 samples distributed as 8 positive and 4 negative. **Model Answer:** **CART (Classification and Regression Trees)** was developed by Breiman, Friedman, Olshen, and Stone in 1984. **CART Algorithm Steps:** 1. Start with the full training set $S$ at the root. 2. For **every** feature and **every** possible binary split point, compute the Gini Gain. 3. Choose the (feature, threshold) pair that **maximizes $\Delta Gini$**. 4. Recursively split each child node. 5. Grow the tree to its **maximum size** (no early stopping by default). 6. Apply **Cost-Complexity Pruning (CCP)** using a validation set to find the optimal $\alpha$. **Key Differences from ID3:** | Aspect | CART | ID3 | |---|---|---| | **Splitting Criterion** | Gini Index | Information Gain | | **Branching** | **Binary** only ($A \leq t$ vs $A > t$) | Multi-way (one branch per attribute value) | | **Output** | **Classification + Regression** | Classification only | | **Pruning** | Cost-Complexity (post-pruning) | None / pre-pruning | | **Continuous Features** | Native support (binary threshold) | Requires pre-discretization | | **Missing Values** | Surrogate splits | Not natively handled | **Gini Index Computation:** Given: 12 samples, $+$ = 8, $-$ = 4. $p_+ = 8/12 = 2/3$, $p_- = 4/12 = 1/3$. $$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_i p_i^2 = 1 - \left[\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^2\right]$$ $$Gini(S) = 1 - \left[\frac{4}{9} + \frac{1}{9}\right] = 1 - \frac{5}{9} = \frac{4}{9}$$ $$\boxed{Gini(S) \approx 0.4444}$$ > [!TIP] > **Verification**: A perfectly balanced binary node ($p_+ = p_- = 0.5$) has the highest Gini for a binary problem: $Gini = 0.5$. Our value $0.444$ is slightly less, which makes sense since the node leans positive (2/3 vs 1/3). **(7 marks: 2 for CART steps, 3 for ID3 vs CART comparison, 2 for Gini computation)** --- **Q. (b)** What is **overfitting** in Decision Trees? Explain **pre-pruning** and **post-pruning** with the **cost-complexity pruning** formula. Why is post-pruning generally preferred? **Model Answer:** **Overfitting in Decision Trees:** A decision tree overfits when it becomes **too complex** — capturing noise and idiosyncrasies of the training set rather than the underlying general pattern. Symptoms: - **Training accuracy ≈ 100%** but **test accuracy is low**. - The tree has **very deep paths** with few samples per leaf. - Highly branching attributes are overused. **Pruning Strategies:** **1. Pre-Pruning (Early Stopping):** Halt tree growth **before** it perfectly fits training data. Common hyperparameter thresholds: - `max_depth`: maximum depth of the tree. - `min_samples_split`: minimum samples required to split a node. - `min_samples_leaf`: minimum samples required at a leaf. - `max_leaf_nodes`: cap on the number of leaves. - `min_impurity_decrease`: minimum IG/Gini decrease to allow a split. **2. Post-Pruning (Reduced-Error / Cost-Complexity Pruning):** Grow the full tree, then **replace subtrees with leaves** if doing so reduces a cost function. **Cost-Complexity Function:** $$R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \cdot \vert L(T) \vert$$ where: - $R(T)$ = misclassification rate (or sum of squared errors for regression) on the validation set. - $\vert L(T) \vert$ = number of leaf nodes (model complexity). - $\alpha \geq 0$ = **complexity parameter** controlling the trade-off. **Algorithm for CCP (Breiman's approach):** 1. Grow the full tree $T_0$. 2. For each internal node, compute the effective $\alpha$ that makes pruning it neutral. 3. Iteratively prune the subtree with the **weakest link** (smallest effective $\alpha$). 4. Generate a sequence of trees $T_0 \supset T_1 \supset T_2 \supset \ldots \supset \{root\}$. 5. Use **cross-validation** to pick the optimal $T_k$ on validation data. **Why Post-Pruning is Generally Preferred:** | Reason | Explanation | |---|---| | **Less myopic** | Pre-pruning may stop too early based on a local greedy decision, missing better splits deeper in the tree. | | **Two-stage wisdom** | Post-pruning first explores **all** possible splits, then prunes — combining the best of both **overfitting avoidance** and **information preservation**. | | **Better generalization** | Empirically, post-pruned trees tend to have **higher test accuracy** for the same training-set size. | | **Optimal subtree** | CCP provably finds the **best-pruned tree** for each $\alpha$ via dynamic programming. | > [!WARNING] > **Drawback of post-pruning**: It is **computationally expensive** because the full tree must be grown first, then evaluated across many $\alpha$ values via cross-validation. For massive datasets, pre-pruning is a practical compromise. **(7 marks: 2 for overfitting definition, 2 for pre/post-pruning, 2 for CCP formula, 1 for post-pruning advantage)** --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Decision Tree Questions** > > 1. **Forgetting the $0 \log 0 = 0$ convention** — when computing entropy on a pure subset ($p_i = 0$ for some class), students often write $\log 0$ which is $-\infty$. Always state the convention explicitly. > 2. **Mixing up Information Gain and Gain Ratio** — IG is the difference of entropies; GR is IG divided by Split Information. Writing one when the question asks for the other costs 2–3 marks. > 3. **Computing conditional entropy with wrong branch weights** — the weights are $\vert S_v \vert / \vert S \vert$, NOT $1/k$ where $k$ is the number of branches. **Branch sizes are usually unequal**. > 4. **Forgetting to convert to base-2 logarithms** — entropy is conventionally in bits, requiring $\log_2$. Using $\ln$ (natural log) gives wrong numerical values. > 5. **Not explaining ID3 vs CART differences** — KTU loves this comparison. A 2-mark answer that just says "CART uses Gini" loses marks vs a structured comparison with branching type, output, and pruning. > 6. **Skipping the recursion** — when asked to "build the tree", students compute IG at the root and stop. You must **recurse** into impure subsets until purity is reached. > 7. **Numerical rounding errors** — keep at least 4 decimal places. Final answers are usually expected to 3 decimal places. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Decision Trees** ### Core Concepts - ✅ A **Decision Tree** is a hierarchical, recursive partition of the feature space using axis-aligned splits. - ✅ Built using a **greedy top-down** strategy (TDIDT / Hunt's Algorithm). - ✅ Used for both **classification** (Decision Tree Classifier) and **regression** (Decision Tree Regressor). ### Impurity Measures - ✅ **Entropy** $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ → maximized at uniform distribution. - ✅ **Gini** $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ → maximized when $p_i = 1/c$ for all $i$. - ✅ **Classification Error** $E(S) = 1 - \max_i p_i$ → rarely used (not sensitive enough). ### Splitting Criteria - ✅ **Information Gain** $IG(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ → used by **ID3**. - ✅ **Gain Ratio** $GR = IG / SI$ → corrects IG's bias toward many-valued attributes → used by **C4.5**. - ✅ **Gini Gain** $\Delta Gini$ → used by **CART** (default in scikit-learn). - ✅ **Variance Reduction** → used by **CART for regression**. ### Algorithm Family - ✅ **ID3** (Quinlan 1986): IG, multi-way, classification only. - ✅ **C4.5** (Quinlan 1993): Gain Ratio, multi-way, handles continuous + missing. - ✅ **CART** (Breiman 1984): Gini, binary splits, classification + regression. ### Overfitting & Pruning - ✅ Overfitting arises when the tree memorizes training noise. - ✅ **Pre-pruning**: limit `max_depth`, `min_samples_leaf`, etc. - ✅ **Post-pruning** (CCP): $R_\alpha(T) = R(T) + \alpha \cdot \vert L(T) \vert$, optimized via cross-validation. ### Continuous & Missing Values - ✅ Continuous → sort and test midpoints as binary thresholds $A \leq t$. - ✅ Missing → surrogate splits (CART), imputation, or probabilistic routing. ### Practical Tips - ✅ Gini is faster (no log) and is the scikit-learn default. - ✅ Decision Trees are **interpretable**, **non-parametric**, and require **no feature scaling**. - ✅ They are **unstable** — small data changes can produce very different trees. **Random Forests** fix this via ensembling. - ✅ Pure leaves have $H = 0$ and $Gini = 0$. - ✅ Information Gain of a feature that perfectly separates classes = root entropy. ### KTU-Specific Quick Reference - ✅ For 3-mark questions: define + formula + role. - ✅ For 7-mark sub-questions: full numerical calculation with all intermediate values shown. - ✅ For 14-mark questions: computation + comparison + algorithm explanation.

topics.title: Model Evaluation Metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET527 - INTRODUCTION TO MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Model Evaluation Metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of supervised Machine Learning, **Model Evaluation Metrics** are quantitative measures used to assess the performance and generalization capability of a trained classification model on unseen (validation/test) data. For binary classification problems, these metrics are derived fundamentally from the **Confusion Matrix** — a tabular layout that cross-tabulates the *Predicted* class labels against the *Actual (Ground Truth)* class labels.

The four cardinal cells of the Confusion Matrix are:

- **TP (True Positives):** Number of positive instances correctly classified as positive.
- **TN (True Negatives):** Number of negative instances correctly classified as negative.
- **FP (False Positives):** Number of negative instances incorrectly classified as positive (Type I Error).
- **FN (False Negatives):** Number of positive instances incorrectly classified as negative (Type II Error).

The four primary scalar metrics evaluated in this module are:

1. **Accuracy** — The proportion of total correct predictions to the total number of predictions.
2. **Precision** — The proportion of correctly predicted positive observations to the total predicted positives.
3. **Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate)** — The proportion of correctly predicted positive observations to all actual positives.
4. **F1-Score** — The weighted harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, balancing both metrics into a single value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students must be able to *construct and interpret* the confusion matrix, *derive* each metric mathematically from first principles, and *justify* the selection of an appropriate metric for a given problem domain (e.g., medical diagnosis vs. spam filtering).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are a **Quality Control Inspector** in a biscuit factory. Your job is to identify *defective* biscuits (Positive class) from a conveyor belt of 1000 biscuits. You have a metal detector that beeps for defective ones.

- **TP (True Positive):** The detector beeps AND the biscuit is genuinely defective. ✅ *Correctly caught.*
- **TN (True Negative):** The detector stays silent AND the biscuit is good. ✅ *Correctly passed.*
- **FP (False Positive):** The detector beeps, but the biscuit is actually fine. ❌ *False alarm — a good biscuit is wrongly rejected.*
- **FN (False Negative):** The detector stays silent, but the biscuit was defective. ❌ *Dangerous miss — a defective biscuit goes to market.*

Now, the metrics translate intuitively:

- **Accuracy** = Out of 1000 biscuits, how many decisions (accept or reject) did I get right overall?
- **Precision** = Out of all the biscuits I *flagged* as defective, how many were *actually* defective? *(Reliability of my alarm)*
- **Recall** = Out of all the *genuinely* defective biscuits in the factory, how many did I *catch*? *(Coverage of the alarm)*
- **F1-Score** = A single balanced grade that punishes you if either Precision OR Recall is poor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use Accuracy?** Consider a rare disease affecting 1 in 10,000 people. A naive model that predicts "No Disease" for *everyone* achieves 99.99% accuracy — yet it is clinically useless because it catches **zero** sick patients. This is the **Class Imbalance Problem**, and it is the central reason why Precision, Recall, and F1-Score exist alongside Accuracy.

## 1.3 The Confusion Matrix — Standard Layout

The 2×2 Confusion Matrix is conventionally arranged with the *Actual* class on the rows and the *Predicted* class on the columns (Kohavi & Provost convention, widely adopted in KTU reference materials):

| | Predicted: **Negative (0)** | Predicted: **Positive (1)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Negative (0)** | **TN** | **FP** |
| **Actual: Positive (1)** | **FN** | **TP** |

> [!NOTE]
> A common alternative arrangement (used in some textbooks) swaps rows and columns. Always verify the orientation before extracting TP, FP, TN, FN. A misread matrix is the **#1 cause of valuation errors** in KTU theory papers.

## 1.4 Notation and Symbols Used Throughout

| Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|
| $N$ | Total number of test instances ($N = TP + TN + FP + FN$) |
| $P$ | Total actual positives ($P = TP + FN$) |
| $\hat{P}$ | Total predicted positives ($\hat{P} = TP + FP$) |
| $N_{neg}$ | Total actual negatives ($N_{neg} = TN + FP$) |
| $\hat{N}_{neg}$ | Total predicted negatives ($\hat{N}_{neg} = TN + FN$) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Confusion Matrix — Cell-by-Cell Logical Decomposition

To evaluate a classifier rigorously, we first construct the confusion matrix. The logical flow is:

1. Run the trained classifier on the test set of size $N$.
2. For each test instance, compare the *predicted label* ($\hat{y}_i$) with the *true label* ($y_i$).
3. Increment the appropriate cell counter:
   - If $y_i = 1$ and $\hat{y}_i = 1$ → increment **TP**.
   - If $y_i = 0$ and $\hat{y}_i = 0$ → increment **TN**.
   - If $y_i = 0$ and $\hat{y}_i = 1$ → increment **FP**.
   - If $y_i = 1$ and $\hat{y}_i = 0$ → increment **FN**.

This four-cell matrix is the **single source of truth** for all downstream scalar metrics.

## 2.2 Accuracy — The "Overall Correctness" Metric

### Formal Definition
Accuracy measures the fraction of predictions that are correct, irrespective of class.

### Mathematical Form
$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{\text{Correct Predictions}}{\text{Total Predictions}}
$$

### Range and Interpretation
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$ (or equivalently $[0\%, 100\%]$)
- **Best Value:** $1$ (perfect classifier)
- **Worst Value:** $0$ (completely wrong on all instances)
- **Limitation:** Highly misleading under class imbalance.

### When to Use
Accuracy is appropriate when:
- The class distribution is roughly balanced (e.g., 50:50 or 60:40).
- The cost of FP and FN are symmetric (i.e., equally bad).
- Example use cases: Handwritten digit recognition (MNIST), general object recognition.

## 2.3 Precision — The "Prediction Reliability" Metric

### Formal Definition
Precision answers the question: *"Of all the instances the model labeled as positive, how many were truly positive?"*

It measures the **quality** of positive predictions.

### Mathematical Form
$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$

### Range and Interpretation
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Best Value:** $1$ (every positive prediction is correct; no false alarms)
- **Worst Value:** $0$ (model flagged positives, but none were actually positive)
- **Also known as:** *Positive Predictive Value (PPV)* in medical statistics.

### When to Use
Precision is the critical metric when the cost of a **False Positive is very high**:
- **Spam Detection:** You do not want a legitimate email ("job offer from Google") classified as spam.
- **Recommendation Systems:** You do not want irrelevant products recommended.
- **Search Engine Ranking:** You do not want irrelevant pages at the top.

## 2.4 Recall (Sensitivity / True Positive Rate) — The "Coverage" Metric

### Formal Definition
Recall answers the question: *"Of all the actual positive instances in the dataset, how many did the model successfully find?"*

It measures the **completeness** of positive detection.

### Mathematical Form
$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$

### Range and Interpretation
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Best Value:** $1$ (every actual positive was detected; no misses)
- **Worst Value:** $0$ (model failed to detect any actual positives)
- **Synonyms:** *Sensitivity*, *True Positive Rate (TPR)*, *Hit Rate*.

### Companion Metric: Specificity (True Negative Rate)
$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP}
$$
This is the "negative class recall" — occasionally asked in KTU theory papers.

### When to Use
Recall is the critical metric when the cost of a **False Negative is very high**:
- **Cancer Diagnosis:** You cannot afford to miss an actual cancer patient.
- **Fraud Detection:** You cannot afford to let fraudulent transactions slip through.
- **Defect Detection in Manufacturing:** A defective product reaching the customer is catastrophic.

## 2.5 The Precision-Recall Trade-off (Critical Concept)

In most real-world binary classifiers (especially probabilistic ones like Logistic Regression that output a score thresholded at 0.5), there is an **inherent tension** between Precision and Recall:

- **Raising the classification threshold** (e.g., from 0.5 to 0.8) → fewer positives are predicted → FP decreases → **Precision increases**, but FN increases → **Recall decreases**.
- **Lowering the threshold** (e.g., from 0.5 to 0.2) → more positives are predicted → FN decreases → **Recall increases**, but FP increases → **Precision decreases**.

This inverse relationship is the famous **Precision-Recall Trade-off**, and is a frequently asked 7-mark question in KTU papers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Precision and Recall can never both be increased simultaneously by changing the threshold. They move in opposite directions. The optimal threshold is chosen based on whether the application values "fewer false alarms" (Precision) or "fewer misses" (Recall) more.

## 2.6 F1-Score — The Harmonic Mean

### Why the Harmonic Mean and not the Arithmetic Mean?

Suppose Precision = 0.9 and Recall = 0.1.
- **Arithmetic Mean:** $(0.9 + 0.1) / 2 = 0.50$ — *misleadingly suggests a decent model.*
- **Harmonic Mean (F1):** $2 \times \frac{0.9 \times 0.1}{0.9 + 0.1} = 0.18$ — *correctly penalizes the imbalance.*

The harmonic mean is **dominated by the smaller of the two values**, which is exactly the property we need: a classifier with skewed Precision and Recall is not a good balanced classifier, and F1 captures this.

### Mathematical Form
$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
$$

### Range and Interpretation
- **Range:** $[0, 1]$
- **Best Value:** $1$ (only when both Precision and Recall are $1$)
- **Worst Value:** $0$ (when either Precision or Recall is $0$)

### Generalization: $F_\beta$-Score
$$
F_\beta = (1 + \beta^2) \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{(\beta^2 \cdot \text{Precision}) + \text{Recall}}
$$
- $\beta = 1$ → Balanced F1 (default).
- $\beta > 1$ → Weights **Recall** more heavily (e.g., $\beta = 2$ for medical diagnosis).
- $\beta < 1$ → Weights **Precision** more heavily (e.g., $\beta = 0.5$ for spam filtering).

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula | Range | Best Value | When to Prioritize |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\frac{TP+TN}{TP+TN+FP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | Balanced classes, symmetric cost |
| **Precision (PPV)** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | FP cost is high (spam filter) |
| **Recall (TPR / Sensitivity)** | $\frac{TP}{TP+FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | FN cost is high (cancer detection) |
| **Specificity (TNR)** | $\frac{TN}{TN+FP}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | Measuring negative class coverage |
| **F1-Score** | $\frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | Need single balanced metric |
| **F1 (alternate form)** | $\frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P+R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | When P and R already computed |
| **$F_\beta$-Score** | $(1+\beta^2) \cdot \frac{P \cdot R}{\beta^2 P + R}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $1$ | Weighted P-R balancing |
| **False Positive Rate (FPR)** | $\frac{FP}{FP+TN} = 1 - \text{Specificity}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ | Used in ROC curves |
| **Error Rate** | $\frac{FP+FN}{TP+TN+FP+FN} = 1 - \text{Accuracy}$ | $[0, 1]$ | $0$ | Complement of accuracy |
| **Prevalence** | $\frac{TP+FN}{N}$ | $[0, 1]$ | — | Class prior; context for metrics |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the KTU 2024 industry-aligned syllabus, these metrics are not just textbook formulas. They are the *de facto* evaluation contract for any deployed ML system. Companies like Google, Amazon, and hospitals use F1-Score (or domain-specific $F_\beta$) to certify models before deployment, especially in safety-critical applications like autonomous driving and medical imaging.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Full Metric Computation

**Problem Statement:**
A binary classifier was tested on $N = 200$ instances. The resulting confusion matrix is:

| | Predicted: **Negative** | Predicted: **Positive** | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Negative** | $TN = 90$ | $FP = 10$ | $100$ |
| **Actual: Positive** | $FN = 20$ | $TP = 80$ | $100$ |
| **Total** | $110$ | $90$ | $200$ |

**Task:** Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, Specificity, and Error Rate.

### Step 1 — Identify the Four Cardinal Values

From the confusion matrix, we read directly:
- $TP = 80$
- $TN = 90$
- $FP = 10$
- $FN = 20$

**Sanity check:** $TP + TN + FP + FN = 80 + 90 + 10 + 20 = 200 = N$. ✓

### Step 2 — Compute Accuracy

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Accuracy} &= \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{80 + 90}{80 + 90 + 10 + 20} \\
&= \frac{170}{200} \\
&= 0.85 \quad \text{or} \quad 85\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The model correctly classified 85% of all instances.

### Step 3 — Compute Precision

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \\
&= \frac{80}{80 + 10} \\
&= \frac{80}{90} \\
&\approx 0.8889 \quad \text{or} \quad 88.89\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** When the model predicts "Positive," it is correct ~88.89% of the time.

### Step 4 — Compute Recall

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Recall} &= \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \\
&= \frac{80}{80 + 20} \\
&= \frac{80}{100} \\
&= 0.80 \quad \text{or} \quad 80\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** The model successfully identified 80% of all actual positive cases, missing 20%.

### Step 5 — Compute F1-Score (Two Equivalent Methods)

**Method A — Using TP, FP, FN directly:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \\
&= \frac{2 \times 80}{2 \times 80 + 10 + 20} \\
&= \frac{160}{160 + 30} \\
&= \frac{160}{190} \\
&\approx 0.8421
\end{aligned}
$$

**Method B — Using Precision and Recall (verification):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.8889 \times 0.80}{0.8889 + 0.80} \\
&= 2 \cdot \frac{0.7111}{1.6889} \\
&\approx 2 \times 0.4211 \\
&\approx 0.8421
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods yield the same F1-Score, confirming the algebraic identity:

$$
\frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} \equiv \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute Specificity (True Negative Rate)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Specificity} &= \frac{TN}{TN + FP} \\
&= \frac{90}{90 + 10} \\
&= \frac{90}{100} \\
&= 0.90 \quad \text{or} \quad 90\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7 — Compute Error Rate

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Error Rate} &= \frac{FP + FN}{N} \\
&= \frac{10 + 20}{200} \\
&= \frac{30}{200} \\
&= 0.15 \quad \text{or} \quad 15\%
\end{aligned}
$$

Or equivalently: $\text{Error Rate} = 1 - \text{Accuracy} = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$. ✓

### Step 8 — Summary Table

| Metric | Value | Percentage |
|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | $0.8500$ | $85.00\%$ |
| Precision | $0.8889$ | $88.89\%$ |
| Recall | $0.8000$ | $80.00\%$ |
| F1-Score | $0.8421$ | $84.21\%$ |
| Specificity | $0.9000$ | $90.00\%$ |
| Error Rate | $0.1500$ | $15.00\%$ |

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Class Imbalance Scenario

**Problem Statement:**
A model screens 10,000 patients for a rare disease. The disease affects 100 people. Confusion matrix:

| | Predicted: **Healthy** | Predicted: **Diseased** | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Healthy** | $TN = 9,890$ | $FP = 10$ | $9,900$ |
| **Actual: Diseased** | $FN = 30$ | $TP = 70$ | $100$ |
| **Total** | $9,920$ | $80$ | $10,000$ |

### Step 1 — Accuracy

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{9890 + 70}{10000} = \frac{9960}{10000} = 0.9960 = 99.60\%
$$

### Step 2 — Precision

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{70}{70 + 10} = \frac{70}{80} = 0.875 = 87.50\%
$$

### Step 3 — Recall

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{70}{70 + 30} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.70 = 70.00\%
$$

### Step 4 — F1-Score

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \times 70}{2 \times 70 + 10 + 30} \\
&= \frac{140}{140 + 40} \\
&= \frac{140}{180} \\
&\approx 0.7778 = 77.78\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Critical Interpretation

> [!WARNING]
> **This example is the KTU 2024 examiner's classic trap.** The model has a stellar **Accuracy of 99.60%** but a relatively poor **Recall of 70%** — meaning **30 diseased patients were told they are healthy**. In medical screening, this is unacceptable. Accuracy alone is *dangerously misleading* in class-imbalanced datasets. This is why F1 (77.78%) gives a far more honest assessment.

**Counter-example to contrast:** A "dummy" classifier that predicts *Healthy* for *every* patient would achieve:
- Accuracy = $9900 / 10000 = 99.00\%$
- Recall = $0 / 100 = 0\%$
- F1-Score = $0$

So a 99% accurate dummy model scores 0 in F1, correctly revealing it as useless for the actual task.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 3 — Threshold Trade-off Demonstration

**Problem Statement:**
A Logistic Regression model outputs the following probability scores for 5 positive and 5 negative test instances.

| Instance | True Label | Predicted Probability |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 (Negative) | $0.05$ |
| 2 | 0 (Negative) | $0.25$ |
| 3 | 0 (Negative) | $0.40$ |
| 4 | 0 (Negative) | $0.55$ |
| 5 | 0 (Negative) | $0.85$ |
| 6 | 1 (Positive) | $0.10$ |
| 7 | 1 (Positive) | $0.45$ |
| 8 | 1 (Positive) | $0.60$ |
| 9 | 1 (Positive) | $0.75$ |
| 10 | 1 (Positive) | $0.95$ |

**Task:** Compute Precision, Recall, and F1 at thresholds $T = 0.5$ and $T = 0.7$.

### Case A — Threshold $T = 0.5$

Predicted Positive if probability $\geq 0.5$, else Negative.

| Instance | True | Prob | Predicted ($\geq 0.5$?) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | $0.05$ | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | $0.25$ | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | $0.40$ | 0 |
| 4 | 0 | $0.55$ | **1** → FP |
| 5 | 0 | $0.85$ | **1** → FP |
| 6 | 1 | $0.10$ | 0 → **FN** |
| 7 | 1 | $0.45$ | 0 → **FN** |
| 8 | 1 | $0.60$ | **1** → TP |
| 9 | 1 | $0.75$ | **1** → TP |
| 10 | 1 | $0.95$ | **1** → TP |

Confusion Matrix at $T = 0.5$:
- $TP = 3$, $FP = 2$, $FN = 2$, $TN = 3$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision}_{0.5} &= \frac{3}{3+2} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 \\
\text{Recall}_{0.5} &= \frac{3}{3+2} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.60 \\
F_{1, 0.5} &= \frac{2 \times 3}{2 \times 3 + 2 + 2} = \frac{6}{10} = 0.60
\end{aligned}
$$

### Case B — Threshold $T = 0.7$

Predicted Positive if probability $\geq 0.7$, else Negative.

| Instance | True | Prob | Predicted ($\geq 0.7$?) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | $0.05$ | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | $0.25$ | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | $0.40$ | 0 |
| 4 | 0 | $0.55$ | 0 |
| 5 | 0 | $0.85$ | **1** → FP |
| 6 | 1 | $0.10$ | 0 → **FN** |
| 7 | 1 | $0.45$ | 0 → **FN** |
| 8 | 1 | $0.60$ | 0 → **FN** |
| 9 | 1 | $0.75$ | **1** → TP |
| 10 | 1 | $0.95$ | **1** → TP |

Confusion Matrix at $T = 0.7$:
- $TP = 2$, $FP = 1$, $FN = 3$, $TN = 4$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Precision}_{0.7} &= \frac{2}{2+1} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667 \\
\text{Recall}_{0.7} &= \frac{2}{2+3} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.40 \\
F_{1, 0.7} &= \frac{2 \times 2}{2 \times 2 + 1 + 3} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.50
\end{aligned}
$$

### Comparative Summary

| Threshold | Precision | Recall | F1-Score |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T = 0.5$ | $0.60$ | $0.60$ | $0.60$ |
| $T = 0.7$ | $0.67$ | $0.40$ | $0.50$ |

**Observations:**
- Raising the threshold from 0.5 to 0.7 increased **Precision** (0.60 → 0.67) but **decreased Recall** (0.60 → 0.40).
- The **F1-Score dropped** (0.60 → 0.50), indicating the model is now *less balanced*.
- This numerically demonstrates the **Precision-Recall Trade-off** in action.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of F1 from Harmonic Mean

The F1-Score is mathematically defined as the **harmonic mean** of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R$).

The harmonic mean of two positive numbers $a$ and $b$ is defined as:

$$
H(a, b) = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}} = \frac{2ab}{a + b}
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $a = P$ and $b = R$:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R}}
$$

**Step 2:** Find a common denominator in the denominator:

$$
\frac{1}{P} + \frac{1}{R} = \frac{R + P}{P \cdot R}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute back:

$$
F_1 = \frac{2}{\frac{P + R}{P \cdot R}} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $P = \frac{TP}{TP+FP}$ and $R = \frac{TP}{TP+FN}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_1 &= \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FP} \cdot \frac{TP}{TP+FN}}{\frac{TP}{TP+FP} + \frac{TP}{TP+FN}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}{TP \left( \frac{1}{TP+FP} + \frac{1}{TP+FN} \right)} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}{TP \cdot \frac{(TP+FN) + (TP+FP)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot \frac{TP^2}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}}{\frac{TP(2 \cdot TP + FP + FN)}{(TP+FP)(TP+FN)}} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot TP^2}{TP(2 \cdot TP + FP + FN)} \\
&= \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN}
\end{aligned}
$$

This confirms the **alternative form** of F1 directly in terms of confusion matrix cells, which is the form most often required in KTU numerical answer sheets.

---

## 3.5 Macro vs. Micro Averaging (Multi-class Preview)

Although the core topic is binary classification, KTU 2024 occasionally references the multi-class extension. For a $C$-class problem:

**Macro-Averaged F1:** Compute F1 for each of the $C$ classes (treating it as the "positive" class in turn), then take the simple arithmetic mean.

$$
F_{1,\text{macro}} = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{i=1}^{C} F_{1,i}
$$

**Micro-Averaged F1:** Aggregate TP, FP, FN across all classes, then compute a single global F1.

$$
F_{1,\text{micro}} = \frac{2 \cdot \sum_i TP_i}{2 \cdot \sum_i TP_i + \sum_i FP_i + \sum_i FN_i}
$$

**When to use which:**
- **Macro-averaging** weights all classes equally → useful when minority class performance matters.
- **Micro-averaging** weights by class frequency → dominated by majority class; tends to equal accuracy in multi-class.

> [!NOTE]
> This sub-topic is often a 2-mark supplementary question. KTU students are expected to know the *concept* and the *formula structure*, not deeply derive it in the exam.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Confusion Matrix Decision Flow

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes how a single test instance flows into one of the four cells of the confusion matrix based on the comparison between its *True Label* and *Predicted Label*.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Test Instance] --> B{Is True Label Positive?}
    B -- "Yes (1)" --> C{Is Predicted Label Positive?}
    B -- "No (0)" --> D{Is Predicted Label Positive?}
    C -- "Yes (1)" --> E[TP: True Positive]
    C -- "No (0)" --> F[FN: False Negative]
    D -- "Yes (1)" --> G[FP: False Positive]
    D -- "No (0)" --> H[TN: True Negative]
    E --> I[End: Increment TP Counter]
    F --> J[End: Increment FN Counter]
    G --> K[End: Increment FP Counter]
    H --> L[End: Increment TN Counter]
```

## 4.2 Metric Derivation Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following diagram maps the *functional flow* of how raw test predictions get transformed into the four scalar metrics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Test Dataset] --> B[Trained Classifier]
    B --> C[Predicted Labels]
    A --> D[True Labels]
    C --> E[Compare Predicted vs True]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Confusion Matrix: TP, FP, TN, FN]
    F --> G1[Accuracy Module: TP+TN over N]
    F --> G2[Precision Module: TP over TP+FP]
    F --> G3[Recall Module: TP over TP+FN]
    G2 --> H[F1 Module: Harmonic Mean of P and R]
    G3 --> H
    G1 --> I[Final Performance Report]
    H --> I
    F --> J[Specificity Module: TN over TN+FP]
    J --> I
```

## 4.3 Precision-Recall Trade-off Schematic

The trade-off is best understood as a threshold-driven movement along a single curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Probabilistic Classifier Output Score] --> B{Choose Threshold T}
    B -- "Low T (e.g., 0.2)" --> C[Many predicted positives]
    C --> C1[Recall goes UP]
    C --> C2[Precision goes DOWN]
    B -- "High T (e.g., 0.8)" --> D[Few predicted positives]
    D --> D1[Precision goes UP]
    D --> D2[Recall goes DOWN]
    B -- "Optimal T (Domain Specific)" --> E[Balanced P and R]
    E --> E1[Maximize F1-Score]
```

## 4.4 Metric Selection Decision Tree (Engineering Application)

This diagram helps a KTU student or practicing engineer decide which metric to use based on the problem context — a common practical exam question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Classification Problem] --> B{Is Dataset Balanced?}
    B -- "Yes" --> C{Is FP cost = FN cost?}
    C -- "Yes" --> D[Use Accuracy]
    C -- "No" --> E{Is FN cost very high?}
    B -- "No (Imbalanced)" --> F{Is FN cost very high?}
    E -- "Yes" --> G[Prioritize Recall, then F1]
    E -- "No" --> H[Prioritize Precision, then F1]
    F -- "Yes" --> G
    F -- "No" --> H
    G --> I[Consider F-beta with beta greater than 1]
    H --> J[Consider F-beta with beta less than 1]
    I --> K[Final: Report F1 or F-beta]
    J --> K
    D --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note for Examiners:** The diagrams above are functional flow architectures, not physical drawings. They directly map the *logical operations* a student must execute to arrive at the correct metric, mirroring the KTU 2024 answer-key evaluation pattern of awarding *process marks* for showing the correct intermediate steps.
