# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the following terms with one example each:**
**(a) Precision**
**(b) Recall**
**(c) F1-Score**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per sub-part):**

**(a) Precision:** Precision is the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to the total predicted positive observations.
$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}
$$
*Example:* In spam filtering, out of 100 emails flagged as spam, 85 are actually spam → Precision = $85/100 = 0.85$.

**(b) Recall:** Recall (also called Sensitivity or True Positive Rate) is the ratio of correctly predicted positive observations to all observations in the actual positive class.
$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN}
$$
*Example:* Out of 100 actual spam emails, the model correctly identified 85 → Recall = $85/100 = 0.85$.

**(c) F1-Score:** F1-Score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a single balanced metric especially useful for imbalanced datasets.
$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}
$$
*Example:* With Precision = 0.85 and Recall = 0.85, $F_1 = 0.85$. With Precision = 0.90 and Recall = 0.10, $F_1 \approx 0.18$ (correctly penalizes the imbalance).

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Explain the limitations of Accuracy as an evaluation metric. Under what conditions is it appropriate to use Accuracy?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Limitations of Accuracy (2 marks):**
1. **Misleading under class imbalance:** When one class heavily dominates, a trivial classifier that always predicts the majority class can achieve deceptively high accuracy. *Example:* In a 99:1 imbalanced dataset, a "predict negative for all" model achieves 99% accuracy despite being completely useless.
2. **Ignores error type asymmetry:** Accuracy treats all misclassifications equally, but in many domains (medical diagnosis, fraud detection), False Negatives are far more costly than False Positives. Accuracy cannot distinguish between them.
3. **Does not reflect class-specific performance:** A model may achieve 90% accuracy overall but only 30% recall on the minority (and often more important) class.

**When Accuracy is Appropriate (1 mark):**
- The dataset is **roughly balanced** (e.g., 50:50 class distribution).
- The costs of **False Positives and False Negatives are symmetric** (equally undesirable).
- *Examples:* MNIST digit classification, balanced sentiment analysis, general image classification with balanced classes.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question B1 (Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Construct the Confusion Matrix from the following classification results of a binary classifier tested on 150 instances: The model predicted "Positive" for 70 instances and "Negative" for 80 instances. Of the 75 actual positive instances, the model correctly identified 60. Compute the **Accuracy**, **Precision**, **Recall**, and **F1-Score** of the classifier. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Precision-Recall Trade-off** in a binary classifier. With a suitable diagram, describe how changing the classification threshold affects Precision and Recall. Compute Precision, Recall, and F1 at two different thresholds for a hypothetical scenario of your choice. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B1(a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Identify the cells of the Confusion Matrix.**

Given:
- Total instances $N = 150$
- Predicted Positive $\hat{P} = 70$ → so Predicted Negative = $150 - 70 = 80$
- Actual Positive $P = 75$ → so Actual Negative = $150 - 75 = 75$
- True Positives $TP = 60$ (correctly identified positives)

**Step 2: Derive remaining cells.**

From $TP + FN = P$:
$$
FN = P - TP = 75 - 60 = 15
$$

From $TP + FP = \hat{P}$:
$$
FP = \hat{P} - TP = 70 - 60 = 10
$$

From $TN + FP = N_{neg}$ (actual negatives = 75):
$$
TN = N_{neg} - FP = 75 - 10 = 65
$$

**Step 3: Construct Confusion Matrix.**

| | Predicted: **Negative** | Predicted: **Positive** | **Total** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Actual: Negative** | $TN = 65$ | $FP = 10$ | $75$ |
| **Actual: Positive** | $FN = 15$ | $TP = 60$ | $75$ |
| **Total** | $80$ | $70$ | $150$ |

**Sanity check:** $65 + 10 + 15 + 60 = 150$. ✓

**Step 4: Compute Accuracy.**

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{60 + 65}{150} = \frac{125}{150} = \frac{5}{6} \approx 0.8333
$$

**Step 5: Compute Precision.**

$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{60}{60 + 10} = \frac{60}{70} = \frac{6}{7} \approx 0.8571
$$

**Step 6: Compute Recall.**

$$
\text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{60}{60 + 15} = \frac{60}{75} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.80
$$

**Step 7: Compute F1-Score.**

$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \times 60}{2 \times 60 + 10 + 15} = \frac{120}{120 + 25} = \frac{120}{145} = \frac{24}{29} \approx 0.8276
$$

**Verification via harmonic mean formula:**

$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{0.8571 \times 0.80}{0.8571 + 0.80} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.6857}{1.6571} \approx 2 \times 0.4138 \approx 0.8276 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Final Tabular Result:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Accuracy | $0.8333$ ($83.33\%$) |
| Precision | $0.8571$ ($85.71\%$) |
| Recall | $0.8000$ ($80.00\%$) |
| F1-Score | $0.8276$ ($82.76\%$) |

---

#### **Solution to B1(b) — 7 Marks**

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

The **Precision-Recall Trade-off** is an inherent inverse relationship in probabilistic binary classifiers (Logistic Regression, SVM with Platt scaling, Neural Networks with sigmoid output, etc.) that produce a continuous score for each instance, which is then converted into a discrete class label by comparing against a **threshold** (default: $0.5$).

- **Precision** measures: *"Of those I declared positive, how many were right?"* (Quality of positive predictions)
- **Recall** measures: *"Of all positives that exist, how many did I find?"* (Coverage of positives)

Because the same threshold governs both decisions, lowering it to catch more positives (raising Recall) inevitably introduces more false alarms (lowering Precision), and vice versa. They move in **opposite directions**.

**Threshold Effects (2 marks):**

| Threshold Behavior | Precision | Recall | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lower threshold** (e.g., 0.3) | ↓ Decreases (more FPs) | ↑ Increases (fewer FNs) | Cancer screening |
| **Higher threshold** (e.g., 0.7) | ↑ Increases (fewer FPs) | ↓ Decreases (more FNs) | Spam filtering |
| **Optimal threshold** | Balanced | Balanced | Maximizes F1 |

**Hypothetical Numerical Demonstration (2 marks):**

Consider a Logistic Regression model with the following 6 test instances:

| Instance | True Label | Predicted Probability |
|---|---|---|
| A | 1 | $0.92$ |
| B | 1 | $0.65$ |
| C | 1 | $0.40$ |
| D | 0 | $0.85$ |
| E | 0 | $0.55$ |
| F | 0 | $0.15$ |

**At threshold $T = 0.5$:**
- Predicted positives: A (0.92), B (0.65), D (0.85), E (0.55) → $TP = 2$ (A, B), $FP = 2$ (D, E), $FN = 1$ (C), $TN = 1$ (F)
- $P = 2/(2+2) = 0.50$
- $R = 2/(2+1) = 0.67$
- $F_1 = 2 \times 0.50 \times 0.67 / (0.50 + 0.67) \approx 0.571$

**At threshold $T = 0.7$:**
- Predicted positives: A (0.92), D (0.85) → $TP = 1$ (A), $FP = 1$ (D), $FN = 2$ (B, C), $TN = 2$ (E, F)
- $P = 1/(1+1) = 0.50$
- $R = 1/(1+2) = 0.33$
- $F_1 = 2 \times 0.50 \times 0.33 / (0.50 + 0.33) \approx 0.40$

**Observation:** Raising threshold from 0.5 to 0.7 changed $P$ from 0.50 to 0.50 (unchanged in this small case) but **drastically reduced Recall** from 0.67 to 0.33 — a textbook demonstration of the trade-off.

---

### **Question B2 (Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** What is the **Confusion Matrix**? Explain its four cells (TP, FP, TN, FN) with a real-world example. For a fraud detection system, why is **Recall more important than Precision**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider a medical diagnosis system that screens 5000 patients for a critical disease. The disease prevalence is 2%. The classifier has **Sensitivity (Recall) = 0.95** and **Specificity = 0.90**. Compute the following:
**(i)** The total number of diseased and non-diseased patients in the test set.
**(ii)** The number of True Positives, False Positives, True Negatives, and False Negatives.
**(iii)** The **Accuracy** and **F1-Score** of the classifier. Take Precision from the derived values.
**(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Solution to B2(a) — 7 Marks**

**Confusion Matrix Definition (2 marks):**

A **Confusion Matrix** (also called an *Error Matrix* or *Contingency Table*) is a 2×2 table that summarizes the performance of a binary classification model by cross-tabulating the **Predicted** class labels (columns) against the **Actual** class labels (rows) on a test dataset. It exposes not only *how many* predictions were correct, but more importantly, *the type* of errors made.

**Four Cells Explained (3 marks):**

Using the example of an **Email Spam Classifier**:

- **TP (True Positive):** The classifier flags an email as Spam, and it *is* actually spam. ✅ *Correct catch.*
  *Example:* A phishing email is correctly identified and moved to the spam folder.
- **TN (True Negative):** The classifier flags an email as Not Spam, and it *is* a legitimate email. ✅ *Correct pass.*
  *Example:* An email from your professor saying "Assignment submission deadline" is correctly delivered to the inbox.
- **FP (False Positive) — Type I Error:** The classifier flags an email as Spam, but it is *actually legitimate*. ❌
  *Example:* An important email from HR about a job offer is wrongly moved to spam, causing you to miss a critical opportunity.
- **FN (False Negative) — Type II Error:** The classifier flags an email as Not Spam, but it *is* actually spam. ❌
  *Example:* A phishing email lands in your inbox, and you click the malicious link, compromising your account.

**Why Recall > Precision in Fraud Detection (2 marks):**

In a **credit card fraud detection system**, the cost asymmetry is severe:

- A **False Negative** (fraudulent transaction classified as legitimate) means the customer *loses real money* and the bank's reputation is damaged. This is a catastrophic, often unrecoverable, error.
- A **False Positive** (legitimate transaction flagged as fraud) is an inconvenience — the customer is asked to verify via OTP, but no money is lost.

Because the **cost of False Negatives is orders of magnitude higher** than False Positives, the system must catch as many fraudulent transactions as possible, even at the cost of a few extra false alarms. This makes **Recall the primary metric** — we want to identify $\geq 99\%$ of all fraud cases, and we tolerate some false positives as an acceptable trade-off.

> [!NOTE]
> Following this reasoning, the F-beta score with $\beta > 1$ (e.g., $F_2$ or $F_3$) is often used in fraud detection, medical diagnosis, and terrorism detection to explicitly weight Recall more heavily than Precision.

---

#### **Solution to B2(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- Total patients $N = 5000$
- Disease prevalence = $2\%$
- Sensitivity (Recall) = $0.95$
- Specificity = $0.90$

**Step (i) — Total Diseased and Non-Diseased Patients (2 marks):**

$$
P = \text{Diseased} = 2\% \times 5000 = 0.02 \times 5000 = 100
$$

$$
N_{neg} = \text{Non-diseased} = 5000 - 100 = 4900
$$

**Step (ii) — Compute TP, FP, TN, FN (3 marks):**

Using Sensitivity:
$$
\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{TP}{P} = \frac{TP}{100} = 0.95
$$

$$
TP = 0.95 \times 100 = 95
$$

$$
FN = P - TP = 100 - 95 = 5
$$

Using Specificity:
$$
\text{Specificity} = \frac{TN}{TN + FP} = \frac{TN}{N_{neg}} = \frac{TN}{4900} = 0.90
$$

$$
TN = 0.90 \times 4900 = 4410
$$

$$
FP = N_{neg} - TN = 4900 - 4410 = 490
$$

**Step (iii) — Compute Accuracy and F1-Score (2 marks):**

**Accuracy:**
$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{N} = \frac{95 + 4410}{5000} = \frac{4505}{5000} = 0.901 = 90.10\%
$$

**Precision:**
$$
\text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{95}{95 + 490} = \frac{95}{585} \approx 0.1624 = 16.24\%
$$

**F1-Score:**
$$
F_1 = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.1624 \times 0.95}{0.1624 + 0.95} = 2 \cdot \frac{0.1543}{1.1124} \approx 2 \times 0.1387 \approx 0.2774
$$

Or directly:
$$
F_1 = \frac{2 \cdot TP}{2 \cdot TP + FP + FN} = \frac{2 \times 95}{2 \times 95 + 490 + 5} = \frac{190}{190 + 495} = \frac{190}{685} \approx 0.2774
$$

**Final Tabular Summary:**

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Diseased ($P$) | $100$ |
| Non-diseased ($N_{neg}$) | $4900$ |
| TP | $95$ |
| FP | $490$ |
| TN | $4410$ |
| FN | $5$ |
| Accuracy | $90.10\%$ |
| Precision | $16.24\%$ |
| Recall | $95.00\%$ |
| F1-Score | $27.74\%$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Critical Observation:** The model has a stellar **Accuracy of 90.10%** and a high **Recall of 95%** (it catches 95 out of 100 sick patients), yet a **terrible Precision of only 16.24%** (only 95 of 585 alarm bells were real). The **F1-Score of 27.74%** is low and honestly reveals the model is *not great* as a stand-alone screening tool. A student who only computes Accuracy and concludes "the model is great" will lose **at least 2 marks** for missing this critical insight. The correct KTU answer must always interpret the metrics *in the context of the application domain*.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps in This Topic:**
> 1. **Reading the Confusion Matrix Backwards:** Many students swap TP with TN or FP with FN. Always verify by asking: *"Does TP = Predicted Positive AND Actually Positive?"* A 14-mark question can yield 0 marks if the matrix is read incorrectly.
> 2. **Forgetting to Compute FN:** A student computes TP, FP, TN, but sets FN = 0, breaking the sanity check $TP + TN + FP + FN = N$. Always verify total.
> 3. **Using Arithmetic Mean instead of Harmonic Mean for F1:** F1 is the **harmonic** mean, not arithmetic. This is a 1-2 mark deduction per KTU valuation key.
> 4. **Confusing Precision with Recall's formula:** Precision uses $TP + FP$ (column sum); Recall uses $TP + FN$ (row sum). Memorize the asymmetry: **P → Predicted column**, **R → Real/Actual row**.
> 5. **Reporting Accuracy without context:** Always discuss *why* a metric is appropriate for the *given problem domain*. Merely computing a number without interpretation loses 1-2 marks.
> 6. **Missing the Class Imbalance Discussion:** If the problem involves imbalanced data and the student computes only Accuracy, this is a partial answer. Always include F1 (or at least Precision and Recall) for imbalanced scenarios.
> 7. **Ignoring Threshold Trade-off:** In 7-mark trade-off questions, students often fail to *numerically demonstrate* the trade-off with an example. Theory alone without numerical evidence is incomplete per KTU 2024 marking schemes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Model Evaluation Metrics**

### **Foundations**
- The **Confusion Matrix** is the single source of all classification metrics.
- Its 4 cells are **TP, FP, TN, FN**. Misreading any one cascades errors through every metric.
- A model with high Accuracy is **not necessarily good** — especially under class imbalance.

### **Core Metrics (Must Memorize)**
- **Accuracy** = $(TP + TN) / N$ → Use only for balanced datasets.
- **Precision** = $TP / (TP + FP)$ → Use when FP cost is high.
- **Recall** = $TP / (TP + FN)$ → Use when FN cost is high.
- **F1-Score** = $2 \cdot TP / (2 \cdot TP + FP + FN)$ → Single balanced metric, especially for imbalanced data.
- **Specificity** = $TN / (TN + FP)$ → The "negative recall."

### **Conceptual Mastery**
- **Precision-Recall Trade-off:** Lowering threshold ↑ Recall, ↓ Precision. Raising threshold ↓ Recall, ↑ Precision. They cannot both be increased by changing the threshold.
- **Why Harmonic Mean for F1?** It is dominated by the smaller of (P, R), so it punishes extreme imbalance. Arithmetic mean would be misleading.
- **$F_\beta$ Generalization:** $\beta > 1$ weights Recall more; $\beta < 1$ weights Precision more; $\beta = 1$ is standard F1.

### **Domain-Appropriate Metric Selection**
- **Cancer / Disease / Fraud Detection** → Recall is king (missing a case is catastrophic).
- **Spam Filtering / Recommendations** → Precision is king (false alarms annoy users).
- **General Balanced Classification** → Accuracy is fine.
- **Imbalanced + Need Single Metric** → F1-Score.

### **Exam Day Quick-Checks**
- Always verify $TP + TN + FP + FN = N$ after computing cells.
- Always specify whether you used the *direct* or *harmonic mean* form of F1 (both give the same answer, but showing the method earns full process marks).
- Always **interpret** the metric values in the context of the problem, not just report numbers.
- For multi-class problems, mention **Macro vs. Micro averaging** as a bonus point.

### **Common Misconceptions to Avoid**
- "Higher Accuracy = Better Model" → **False** under class imbalance.
- "Precision and Recall are independent" → **False**; they are inversely related via threshold.
- "F1 is the same as Accuracy" → **False**; F1 only considers the positive class, Accuracy considers both.
- "A 99% accurate model is excellent" → **Possibly false**; check Precision/Recall on minority class.

> **End of KTU Module 4 Notes — Model Evaluation Metrics: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Support Vector Machines (SVM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET527 - INTRODUCTION TO MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Support Vector Machines (SVM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised machine learning algorithm used for **classification** and **regression** that finds the **optimal separating hyperplane** which maximizes the **margin** between two classes in a feature space. The data points that lie closest to the decision boundary and directly influence its position are termed **Support Vectors**.

Mathematically, for a binary classification problem with training set $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, SVM seeks a hyperplane:

$$
w^T x + b = 0
$$

such that the **margin** $\dfrac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$ is maximized, and the dual optimization using the **Kernel Trick** allows non-linear classification in the original input space.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Widest Road Between Two Villages":**
> Imagine two villages (Class +1 and Class −1) separated by a river. You are the town planner asked to build a single straight road (the hyperplane) such that the gap (margin) between the road and the nearest house on either side is as **wide** as possible. The houses closest to the road are the **support vectors** — they "support" and define the road's position. If you move a house far away, the road doesn't budge; if you move a support-vector house even slightly, the entire road shifts. SVM is this: build the **widest possible empty street** between two classes.

### 1.3 Key Vocabulary (KTU Board Terminology)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| Hyperplane | $w^T x + b = 0$ | The decision boundary |
| Weight vector | $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$ | Normal to the hyperplane |
| Bias | $b \in \mathbb{R}$ | Offset of hyperplane from origin |
| Margin | $\frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$ | Width of the separating street |
| Support Vector | $x_i$ | Training point lying on the margin boundary |
| Slack variable | $\xi_i \geq 0$ | Penalty for misclassification (soft margin) |
| Penalty parameter | $C > 0$ | Trade-off between margin width and misclassification |

### 1.4 Why SVM Matters in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **SVM is dominant in high-dimensional, low-sample-size problems** such as:
> - **Biomedical**: Cancer/tumor classification from gene expression data.
> - **Image Processing**: Handwritten digit recognition, face detection.
> - **NLP**: Text categorization, spam filtering.
> - **Fault Diagnosis**: Industrial machinery failure classification.
> - **Bioinformatics**: Protein secondary structure prediction.
> - **Cybersecurity**: Intrusion detection systems.
> - **Remote Sensing**: Land-cover classification from satellite imagery.

The hallmark is that SVM generalizes well even with limited training data because it depends only on support vectors — not the entire dataset.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of SVM (Structured Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why and How' Behind Every Step**

1. **STEP 1 — Represent the decision boundary as a hyperplane**
   * *Why*: A linear separator in $d$-dimensional space is a $(d-1)$-dimensional affine surface — the simplest geometric structure that can split two classes.
   * *How*: Parametrize it with a normal vector $w$ and a bias $b$ so that points on one side give $w^T x + b > 0$ and the other give $w^T x + b < 0$.

2. **STEP 2 — Classify using the sign of the discriminant**
   * *Why*: A linear classifier must produce $\pm 1$ outputs.
   * *How*: $\hat{y} = \text{sign}(w^T x + b)$.

3. **STEP 3 — Define margin as the perpendicular distance to the nearest point**
   * *Why*: A wider margin yields better generalization (lower VC dimension).
   * *How*: Distance from $x_i$ to the hyperplane is $\dfrac{\vert w^T x_i + b \vert}{\lVert w \rVert}$.

4. **STEP 4 — Constrain the canonical hyperplane so that the closest point lies at distance $\frac{1}{\lVert w \rVert}$**
   * *Why*: Scaling invariance of the hyperplane equation lets us fix the scale.
   * *How*: Impose $y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ for all $i$.

5. **STEP 5 — Maximize the margin by minimizing $\lVert w \rVert^2$**
   * *Why*: Margin $= \dfrac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$ — maximizing it is equivalent to minimizing the squared norm.
   * *How*: Solve a **Quadratic Programming (QP)** problem.

6. **STEP 6 — Introduce slack variables $\xi_i$ for non-separable data (Soft Margin)**
   * *Why*: Real data is rarely perfectly linearly separable.
   * *How*: Allow some points to violate the margin at a penalty $C \xi_i$.

7. **STEP 7 — Apply the Kernel Trick for non-linear separation**
   * *Why*: Many real-world classes cannot be separated by a straight line.
   * *How*: Replace inner products $x_i^T x_j$ with $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$ computed in high-dimensional space without explicit mapping.

8. **STEP 8 — Use KKT conditions to identify support vectors**
   * *Why*: Only points with non-zero Lagrangian multipliers affect the solution.
   * *How*: $0 < \alpha_i < C$ in the soft-margin case identifies the support vectors.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mathematically verified and are board-examination essentials.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Decision Hyperplane | $w^T x + b = 0$ | $w \in \mathbb{R}^d$, $b \in \mathbb{R}$ |
| 2 | Perpendicular Distance | $\dfrac{\vert w^T x_i + b \vert}{\lVert w \rVert}$ | Geometric margin of a point |
| 3 | Functional Margin | $y_i (w^T x_i + b)$ | Signed margin (not normalized) |
| 4 | Geometric Margin | $\dfrac{y_i (w^T x_i + b)}{\lVert w \rVert}$ | True margin of a point |
| 5 | **Hard-Margin Objective** | $\min_{w,b} \dfrac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2$ | Subject to $y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$ |
| 6 | **Soft-Margin Primal** | $\min_{w,b,\xi} \dfrac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$ | Subject to $y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$, $\xi_i \geq 0$ |
| 7 | Lagrangian (Primal) | $L = \dfrac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2 - \sum_i \alpha_i [y_i(w^T x_i + b) - 1]$ | $\alpha_i \geq 0$ |
| 8 | Dual Objective | $\max_{\alpha} \sum_i \alpha_i - \dfrac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j x_i^T x_j$ | Subject to $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$, $\alpha_i \geq 0$ |
| 9 | Weight Recovery | $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ | Sparse — only $\alpha_i > 0$ contribute |
| 10 | Decision Function | $f(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_i \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b\right)$ | Kernelized form |
| 11 | Hinge Loss | $\ell(y, f(x)) = \max(0,\, 1 - y\, f(x))$ | Surrogate 0–1 loss |
| 12 | **Linear Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i^T x_j$ | No transformation |
| 13 | **Polynomial Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = (\gamma x_i^T x_j + r)^d$ | Degree $d$, coefficient $r$ |
| 14 | **RBF (Gaussian) Kernel** | $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2)$ | Infinite-dimensional feature space |
| 15 | Sigmoid Kernel | $K(x_i, x_j) = \tanh(\gamma x_i^T x_j + r)$ | Not always a valid Mercer kernel |
| 16 | VC Dimension (Hard Margin) | $h \leq \min\!\left(N,\, \dfrac{R^2}{\rho^2}\right) + 1$ | $R$ = radius, $\rho$ = margin |
| 17 | Slack Penalty Trade-off | $C \uparrow \Rightarrow$ narrower margin, fewer violations | Misclassification cost |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **In the field**, SVMs are favoured over deep nets when data is scarce but feature dimensions are high. Examples in KTU-relevant engineering:
> - **Fault classification in induction motors** — vibration sensor features.
> - **EEG-based seizure detection** — biomedical signal processing.
> - **Bridge structural health monitoring** — strain gauge time-series features.
> - **Email spam filtering** — bag-of-words features.
> - **Crop disease identification** from leaf images.
> - **Handwritten Malayalam/English character recognition** in OCR pipelines.

The **kernel trick** allows SVMs to learn non-linear boundaries without ever computing coordinates in the transformed (possibly infinite-dimensional) space — a concept central to **Kernel Methods in Computational Learning Theory**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I: Margin as $\dfrac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Prove that the perpendicular distance between the two canonical margin hyperplanes equals $\frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$.

Consider two parallel hyperplanes that just touch the support vectors on either side:

$$
w^T x + b = +1 \quad \text{(positive margin boundary)}
$$

$$
w^T x + b = -1 \quad \text{(negative margin boundary)}
$$

The perpendicular distance from any point $x$ to the hyperplane $w^T x + b = 0$ is given by the standard point-to-plane formula in $\mathbb{R}^d$:

$$
d = \frac{\vert w^T x + b \vert}{\lVert w \rVert}
$$

The distance from the positive margin boundary $w^T x + b = 1$ to the central hyperplane is obtained by substituting a point on that boundary — for instance, take $x_+$ such that $w^T x_+ + b = 1$:

$$
d_+ = \frac{\vert 1 \vert}{\lVert w \rVert} = \frac{1}{\lVert w \rVert}
$$

Similarly, the distance from the negative margin boundary $w^T x + b = -1$ is:

$$
d_- = \frac{\vert -1 \vert}{\lVert w \rVert} = \frac{1}{\lVert w \rVert}
$$

Therefore, the total width of the margin (the "street") between the two support-vector boundaries is:

$$
\text{Margin} = d_+ + d_- = \frac{1}{\lVert w \rVert} + \frac{1}{\lVert w \rVert} = \frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}
$$

This is the **Geometric Margin**. Maximizing $\frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$ is equivalent to minimizing $\lVert w \rVert$, and by convexity, we minimize the equivalent convex objective $\frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2$.

### 3.2 Derivation II: Soft-Margin Primal Problem (Lagrangian Setup)

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Set up the constrained optimization for soft-margin SVM via Lagrangian multipliers.

The soft-margin optimization problem is:

$$
\min_{w, b, \xi} \; \frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

subject to the constraints:

$$
y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad \forall i = 1, \dots, N
$$

We introduce **Lagrangian multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$ for the first set of constraints and $\mu_i \geq 0$ for $\xi_i \geq 0$. The primal Lagrangian is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) &= \frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \\
&\quad - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] \\
&\quad - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \xi_i
\end{aligned}
$$

The KKT stationarity conditions (setting partial derivatives to zero) are:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C
$$

The last condition $\alpha_i \leq C$ emerges because $\mu_i \geq 0$ and $C = \alpha_i + \mu_i$, so $\alpha_i \leq C$. This is a critical KTU 2024 result distinguishing the soft-margin box constraint from the hard-margin $\alpha_i \geq 0$ alone.

### 3.3 Derivation III: Dual Problem via Substitution

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Substitute the stationarity conditions back into $\mathcal{L}$ to obtain the dual.

Substituting $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ into $\frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 &= \frac{1}{2} w^T w = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i \right)^T \left( \sum_j \alpha_j y_j x_j \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^T x_j
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $w$ and the constraint $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ (so the $b$-term vanishes) into the Lagrangian:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}_D(\alpha) &= \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^T x_j \\
&\quad + \sum_i \xi_i (C - \alpha_i - \mu_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

The $\xi_i$-term vanishes because of the stationarity condition $C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0$. We are left with the **Wolfe dual**:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^T x_j
$$

subject to:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0, \quad 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C, \quad \forall i
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The dual depends on data **only through inner products** $x_i^T x_j$. This is what enables the **kernel substitution** $x_i^T x_j \to K(x_i, x_j)$.

### 3.4 Derivation IV: KKT Complementarity Conditions

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Identify which training points become support vectors.

The complementarity slackness conditions are:

$$
\alpha_i \left[ y_i (w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] = 0, \quad \forall i
$$

$$
\mu_i \, \xi_i = 0, \quad \forall i
$$

**Case Analysis (highly important for KTU boards):**

* If $\alpha_i = 0$: The point $x_i$ is **not** a support vector. It lies strictly outside the margin (or correctly classified beyond the margin).

* If $0 < \alpha_i < C$: The point $x_i$ lies **exactly on the margin**, i.e., $y_i (w^T x_i + b) = 1$. This is a **free support vector**.

* If $\alpha_i = C$: The point $x_i$ violates the margin or is misclassified. The slack $\xi_i > 0$. This is a **bounded support vector**.

Thus, in practice, only a **sparse subset** of training points influences the decision boundary — these are the **support vectors**. This sparsity is the source of SVM's computational efficiency.

### 3.5 Derivation V: Kernel Trick and Mercer's Condition

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Justify the kernel substitution $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$.

Let $\phi: \mathbb{R}^d \to \mathcal{H}$ be a mapping into a (possibly infinite-dimensional) Hilbert space $\mathcal{H}$. The dual objective becomes:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \; \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)
$$

For computational tractability, we define the **kernel function**:

$$
K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)
$$

By **Mercer's Theorem**, a symmetric function $K: \mathcal{X} \times \mathcal{X} \to \mathbb{R}$ is a valid kernel (i.e., can be expressed as an inner product in some $\mathcal{H}$) if and only if for any finite set $\{x_1, \dots, x_N\}$, the **Gram matrix** $G_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is **positive semi-definite**:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} c_i c_j K(x_i, x_j) \geq 0, \quad \forall c \in \mathbb{R}^N
$$

The decision function in kernel form is:

$$
f(x) = \text{sign}\!\left( \sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b \right)
$$

### 3.6 Numerical Example: Hard-Margin SVM for 2-D Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example** (commonly asked in KTU exams).

Consider four 2-D training points: $x_1 = (1, 1), y_1 = +1$; $x_2 = (2, 2), y_2 = +1$; $x_3 = (1, 0), y_3 = -1$; $x_4 = (0, 1), y_4 = -1$. Find the optimal separating hyperplane.

By symmetry, the hyperplane passes through the origin, so $b = 0$. The support vectors will be the points closest to the boundary. We solve:

$$
\min_{w} \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2
$$

subject to $y_i (w^T x_i) \geq 1$. Trying $x_1$ and $x_3$ as support vectors:

$$
w_1 + w_2 = 1, \quad -(w_1) = 1 \;\Rightarrow\; w_1 = -1, \; w_2 = 2
$$

Check $x_2$: $(-1)(2) + (2)(2) = 2 \geq 1$ ✓. Check $x_4$: $-(-1)(0) - (2)(1) = -2 < 1$ ✗ — violates the constraint. So $x_4$ is also a support vector. Solve with three support vectors: $w = \alpha_1 y_1 x_1 + \alpha_2 y_2 x_2 + \alpha_4 y_4 x_4$. By KKT and the equality $\sum \alpha_i y_i = 0$:

$$
\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 - \alpha_4 = 0, \quad \text{with } \alpha_1 = \alpha_2 = \alpha_4 = \frac{1}{2} \text{ (by symmetry of margin)}.
$$

This gives $w = \frac{1}{2}(1,1) - \frac{1}{2}(2,2) - \frac{1}{2}(0,1) = (-\frac{1}{2}, -1)$, and the hyperplane is $-\frac{1}{2} x_1 - x_2 = 0$, i.e., $x_1 + 2x_2 = 0$. Margin $= \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Boards:** The expected student answer should always list: support vectors, optimal $w$, $b$, margin width, and classification rule.

### 3.7 Multi-Class SVM Strategies

> [!NOTE]
> **Multi-class is a common 7-mark question.**

| Strategy | Idea | # Classifiers | Pros | Cons |
|----------|------|---------------|------|------|
| **One-vs-All (OvA)** | Train $K$ SVMs, each separating class $k$ from rest | $K$ | Simple, fast training | Class imbalance |
| **One-vs-One (OvO)** | Train $\binom{K}{2}$ SVMs, one per pair | $\frac{K(K-1)}{2}$ | Balanced subproblems | Many classifiers; voting needed |
| **DAGSVM** | Directed Acyclic Graph variant of OvO | $\frac{K(K-1)}{2}$ | Faster test time | Complex training |
| **Crammer-Singer** | Single optimization with multi-class loss | $1$ | Theoretically clean | Heavy optimization |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SVM Decision Boundary Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Training Data set of Xi Yi pairs] --> B[Data is Linearly Separable?]
    B -- Yes --> C[Hard Margin SVM]
    B -- No --> D[Soft Margin SVM with slack variables and penalty C]
    C --> E[Formulate Primal Optimization: minimize 1 over 2 times norm w squared]
    D --> F[Formulate Soft Primal: minimize 1 over 2 norm w squared plus C times sum of slack]
    E --> G[Construct Lagrangian with multipliers alpha i]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Derive Wolfe Dual Problem: maximize sum alpha minus 1 over 2 sum sum alpha i alpha j y i y j K of x i and x j]
    H --> I{Kernel Trick Applied?}
    I -- No --> J[Use Linear Kernel: K of x i and x j equals inner product]
    I -- Yes --> K[Choose Kernel: RBF, Polynomial, or Sigmoid]
    J --> L[Solve QP Problem to obtain alpha i and b]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Identify Support Vectors where alpha i greater than 0]
    M --> N[Construct Decision Function f of x equals sign of weighted sum plus bias]
    N --> O[Classify New Test Point]
```

### 4.2 SVM Concept Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SVM[Support Vector Machine] --> HYP[Hyperplane Definition]
    SVM --> MAR[Margin Maximization]
    SVM --> SVP[Support Vectors]
    SVM --> KER[Kernel Trick]
    SVM --> SOF[Soft Margin with Slack]
    HYP --> EQ1[Equation: w transpose x plus b equals 0]
    MAR --> EQ2[Margin equals 2 divided by norm w]
    SVP --> EQ3[Points on margin boundary: y i times w transpose x i plus b equals 1]
    KER --> RBF[RBF Kernel: exp of negative gamma times squared distance]
    KER --> POL[Polynomial Kernel: gamma times inner product plus r raised to d]
    SOF --> SLK[Slack variables xi i and penalty parameter C]
    SOF --> KKT[KKT Box Constraint: 0 less than or equal to alpha i less than or equal to C]
```

### 4.3 SVM Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[Training Module] --> T2[Feature Scaling: Standardization or Min-Max]
    T2 --> T3[Kernel Matrix Computation: K of x i and x j]
    T3 --> T4[Quadratic Programming Solver: SMO or CVX]
    T4 --> T5[Lagrangian Multipliers alpha i and Bias b]
    T5 --> T6[Support Vector Identification]
    T6 --> T7[Model Storage: alpha i, SVs, b, kernel params]
    P1[Prediction Module] --> P2[New Input Vector x test]
    P2 --> P3[Kernel Distance: K of x test and x sv for all SVs]
    T7 --> P3
    P3 --> P4[Compute f of x test equals sign of sum alpha i y i K plus b]
    P4 --> P5[Output Class Label]
```

### 4.4 KKT Conditions and Support Vector Classification (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Training Point x i] --> B{Value of alpha i}
    B -- alpha i equals 0 --> C1[Non Support Vector: lies beyond margin]
    B -- alpha i between 0 and C --> C2[Free Support Vector: exactly on margin]
    B -- alpha i equals C --> C3[Bounded Support Vector: violates margin or misclassified]
    C1 --> D[No contribution to w]
    C2 --> E[Contributes to w: w equals sum alpha i y i x i]
    C3 --> E
    E --> F[Final Decision Boundary]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024):** Each module typically carries **20 marks** in Part A (3 marks × ~6) and the same in Part B (14 marks × 1–2 with internal choice). Practice problems below mirror that pattern.

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a Support Vector Machine. What are support vectors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised learning algorithm that constructs an optimal separating hyperplane in the feature space to classify data into two categories. It is optimal in the sense that the margin — the distance from the hyperplane to the nearest training points — is maximized.

**Support vectors** are the training data points that lie closest to the decision hyperplane, i.e., on the margin boundaries defined by $w^T x + b = +1$ and $w^T x + b = -1$. They are the only points with non-zero Lagrangian multipliers $\alpha_i$ and are solely responsible for defining the decision boundary. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for mathematical condition, 1 mark for the role of support vectors.)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State and explain the kernel trick in SVM.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **kernel trick** is a method that enables SVMs to perform non-linear classification by implicitly mapping the input data into a higher-dimensional feature space using a kernel function $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$, without ever computing the coordinates of $\phi(x)$ explicitly.

Common kernels include:
- **Linear:** $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i^T x_j$ *(1 mark)*
- **Polynomial:** $K(x_i, x_j) = (\gamma x_i^T x_j + r)^d$ *(0.5 mark)*
- **RBF (Gaussian):** $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2)$ *(0.5 mark)*

By Mercer's theorem, $K$ must correspond to a positive semi-definite Gram matrix to be valid. The kernel trick allows SVMs to learn complex non-linear boundaries at the same computational cost as the linear case. *(1 mark for the trick itself.)*

---

### 📗 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Formulate the primal optimization problem for a soft-margin SVM. Explain the role of the penalty parameter $C$ and slack variables $\xi_i$. How does the soft-margin formulation reduce to the hard-margin case? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Primal Formulation (3 marks):**

For training data $\{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$ that is **not perfectly linearly separable**, the soft-margin SVM primal is:

$$
\min_{w, b, \xi} \; \frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i
$$

subject to the constraints:

$$
y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \xi_i \geq 0, \quad i = 1, \dots, N
$$

**Step 2 — Role of Slack Variables $\xi_i$ (2 marks):**

The slack variable $\xi_i$ measures the degree of margin violation by the $i$-th training point:
- $\xi_i = 0$: Point is correctly classified and outside or on the margin.
- $0 < \xi_i \leq 1$: Point lies inside the margin but is still correctly classified.
- $\xi_i > 1$: Point is **misclassified**.

The term $\sum_i \xi_i$ is the total margin violation, which the optimizer tries to keep small.

**Step 3 — Role of $C$ (1 mark):**

The penalty parameter $C > 0$ controls the **trade-off** between margin width and classification errors:
- **Large $C$**: Heavily penalizes misclassifications → narrower margin, fewer violations (risk of overfitting).
- **Small $C$**: Allows more violations → wider margin, better generalization (risk of underfitting).

**Step 4 — Reduction to Hard-Margin (1 mark):**

As $C \to \infty$, the optimizer cannot tolerate any $\xi_i > 0$, forcing all $\xi_i = 0$. The constraints reduce to $y_i (w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$, which is the hard-margin case. Conversely, $C \to 0$ ignores the data and degenerates.

---

**(b) Derive the dual optimization problem from the soft-margin primal using the Lagrangian. Clearly state the KKT conditions and identify the support vectors. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lagrangian Construction (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L}(w, b, \xi, \alpha, \mu) &= \frac{1}{2} \lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i \\
&\quad - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (w^T x_i + b) - 1 + \xi_i \right] \\
&\quad - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mu_i \xi_i
\end{aligned}
$$

with $\alpha_i \geq 0$, $\mu_i \geq 0$.

**Step 2 — Stationarity (KKT Stationarity) (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial w} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; w = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i x_i
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial b} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \xi_i} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; C - \alpha_i - \mu_i = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C
$$

**Step 3 — Dual Derivation (2 marks):**

Substituting stationarity back and using $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ (the $b$-term vanishes), we get the **Wolfe dual**:

$$
\max_{\alpha} \; \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j \, x_i^T x_j
$$

subject to $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.

**Step 4 — Support Vector Identification (1 mark):**

By **complementary slackness**:
- $\alpha_i = 0$ → not a support vector.
- $0 < \alpha_i < C$ → free support vector on the margin.
- $\alpha_i = C$ → bounded support vector, inside or across the margin.

The decision function is $f(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b\right)$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the kernel trick in detail. Derive the expression for the RBF kernel and show how it leads to an infinite-dimensional feature space. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**

The dual SVM depends on data only through inner products $x_i^T x_j$. If we can replace this inner product with $K(x_i, x_j) = \phi(x_i)^T \phi(x_j)$ for some non-linear $\phi$, the classifier becomes non-linear in the input space but linear in $\phi$-space.

**Step 2 — Mercer's Condition (1 mark):**

$K$ is a valid kernel iff for any finite set $\{x_1, \dots, x_N\}$, the Gram matrix $G_{ij} = K(x_i, x_j)$ is **positive semi-definite**.

**Step 3 — Taylor Expansion of RBF (3 marks):**

The RBF kernel is:

$$
K(x_i, x_j) = \exp\!\left( -\gamma \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2 \right)
$$

Expand $\lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2 = \lVert x_i \rVert^2 + \lVert x_j \rVert^2 - 2 x_i^T x_j$:

$$
K(x_i, x_j) = \exp\!\left( -\gamma \lVert x_i \rVert^2 \right) \exp\!\left( -\gamma \lVert x_j \rVert^2 \right) \exp\!\left( 2\gamma x_i^T x_j \right)
$$

Using the Taylor series $\exp(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{z^k}{k!}$ with $z = 2\gamma x_i^T x_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
K(x_i, x_j) &= \exp\!\left( -\gamma \lVert x_i \rVert^2 \right) \exp\!\left( -\gamma \lVert x_j \rVert^2 \right) \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{(2\gamma)^k (x_i^T x_j)^k}{k!} \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \phi_k(x_i) \, \phi_k(x_j)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\phi_k(x) = \dfrac{\sqrt{(2\gamma)^k}}{\sqrt{k!}} \exp(-\gamma \lVert x \rVert^2) \, P_k(x)$ for some polynomial $P_k$ of degree $k$.

**Step 4 — Infinite-Dimensional Feature Space (2 marks):**

The summation index $k$ runs from $0$ to $\infty$, meaning $\phi$ maps inputs to an **infinite-dimensional** Hilbert space. Yet, the kernel value $K(x_i, x_j)$ is computable in closed form in finite time — this is the essence of the **kernel trick**. No actual coordinates in the infinite space need to be computed; only inner products are evaluated.

---

**(b) Compare One-vs-All and One-vs-One strategies for multi-class SVM. Discuss their pros, cons, and a numerical example with 4 classes. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — One-vs-All (OvA) (2 marks):**

For $K$ classes, train $K$ binary SVMs. The $k$-th SVM separates class $k$ from "all the rest." Classification: assign $x$ to the class with the highest decision value:

$$
\hat{y}(x) = \arg\max_{k \in \{1, \dots, K\}} \; w_k^T x + b_k
$$

**Step 2 — One-vs-One (OvO) (2 marks):**

For $K$ classes, train $\binom{K}{2} = \dfrac{K(K-1)}{2}$ binary SVMs, one for each pair. Classification: use **majority voting** over all pairwise predictions.

**Step 3 — Comparison Table (1.5 marks):**

| Aspect | One-vs-All | One-vs-One |
|--------|------------|------------|
| No. of classifiers | $K$ | $K(K-1)/2$ |
| Training time | Lower | Higher |
| Class imbalance | Suffers (1 vs $K-1$) | Balanced (1 vs 1) |
| Test time | Faster ($K$ evaluations) | Slower ($\binom{K}{2}$ votes) |
| Ties | None directly | Possible (resolved by voting) |

**Step 4 — Numerical Example for $K = 4$ (1.5 marks):**

* OvA: Train 4 SVMs — C1 vs {C2, C3, C4}, C2 vs {C1, C3, C4}, etc.
* OvO: Train $\binom{4}{2} = 6$ SVMs — (C1,C2), (C1,C3), (C1,C4), (C2,C3), (C2,C4), (C3,C4). For a test point, collect 6 votes and assign the class with majority.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in SVM questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ in $\frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2$** — The factor ½ is convention for mathematical convenience; do not drop it.
> 2. **Writing the dual objective sign wrong** — It is a *maximization* of $\sum \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum \sum \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$, not a minimization.
> 3. **Omitting the box constraint** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ in the soft-margin dual — this is what distinguishes it from the hard-margin case ($\alpha_i \geq 0$ only).
> 4. **Mixing up hard and soft margin** — Hard margin is for perfectly separable data; soft margin uses slack $\xi_i$ and penalty $C$.
> 5. **Confusing support vectors with all training points** — Only points with $\alpha_i > 0$ are support vectors.
> 6. **Neglecting bias $b$** — Some students drop the bias term; it is essential for an affine (not just linear) hyperplane.
> 7. **Stating kernel trick without Mercer's condition** — Board examiners expect the validity criterion.
> 8. **Skipping the geometric margin derivation** — The relation $\text{Margin} = \frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$ is a 3-mark standalone question if asked.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam!)**

- ✅ **SVM Objective (Hard Margin):** $\min \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2$ subject to $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1$.
- ✅ **SVM Objective (Soft Margin):** $\min \frac{1}{2}\lVert w \rVert^2 + C \sum_i \xi_i$ subject to $y_i(w^T x_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$, $\xi_i \geq 0$.
- ✅ **Geometric Margin:** $\frac{2}{\lVert w \rVert}$ — maximize margin ⇔ minimize $\lVert w \rVert$.
- ✅ **Weight Recovery:** $w = \sum_i \alpha_i y_i x_i$ — only support vectors (with $\alpha_i > 0$) contribute.
- ✅ **Dual Form:** $\max_\alpha \sum_i \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i,j} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(x_i, x_j)$ s.t. $\sum_i \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$.
- ✅ **Decision Function:** $f(x) = \text{sign}\!\left(\sum_{i \in SV} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b\right)$.
- ✅ **KKT Box:** $0 \leq \alpha_i \leq C$ in soft margin; $\alpha_i \geq 0$ in hard margin.
- ✅ **Support Vector Types:** Non-SV ($\alpha_i = 0$), Free SV ($0 < \alpha_i < C$), Bounded SV ($\alpha_i = C$).
- ✅ **Linear Kernel:** $K(x_i, x_j) = x_i^T x_j$.
- ✅ **Polynomial Kernel:** $K(x_i, x_j) = (\gamma x_i^T x_j + r)^d$.
- ✅ **RBF (Gaussian) Kernel:** $K(x_i, x_j) = \exp(-\gamma \lVert x_i - x_j \rVert^2)$ — infinite-dimensional.
- ✅ **Sigmoid Kernel:** $K(x_i, x_j) = \tanh(\gamma x_i^T x_j + r)$ — *not always Mercer-valid*.
- ✅ **Hinge Loss:** $\ell(y, f(x)) = \max(0, 1 - y f(x))$ — convex upper bound on 0–1 loss.
- ✅ **Mercer's Theorem:** A valid kernel needs a positive semi-definite Gram matrix.
- ✅ **Multi-class Strategies:** OvA (K classifiers), OvO (K(K−1)/2 classifiers), DAGSVM, Crammer–Singer.
- ✅ **VC Dimension Bound (Hard Margin):** $h \leq \min(N, R^2/\rho^2) + 1$ — larger margin → smaller VC dimension.
- ✅ **Effect of $C$:** Large $C$ → narrow margin, fewer violations; small $C$ → wide margin, more violations.
- ✅ **Sparsity Property:** Only support vectors define the boundary — the entire training set is *not* required at test time.
- ✅ **Why Kernel Trick Works:** The dual depends on data only via inner products $x_i^T x_j$, so we can replace them with $K(x_i, x_j)$ — this is the central insight enabling non-linear SVMs.

> 🎓 **Final Note:** SVM is conceptually elegant because it reduces a classification problem to a **convex quadratic program** with a unique global optimum. Master the **primal–dual relationship**, the **KKT conditions**, and the **kernel trick** — these three pillars account for ~70% of all KTU SVM questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ensemble Methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET527 - INTRODUCTION TO MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ensemble Methods (Random Forest, Gradient Boosting)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Ensemble Learning?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)**
> **Ensemble Learning** is a machine learning paradigm where multiple *base learners* (also called *weak learners* or *base models*) are strategically combined to solve a particular computational intelligence problem, with the objective of producing a single **optimal predictive model** that outperforms any individual constituent learner. Mathematically, an ensemble approximates a target function $f(x)$ via a weighted aggregation of hypotheses: $H(x) = \mathcal{F}\left(h_1(x), h_2(x), \ldots, h_M(x)\right)$.

Where:
- $h_i(x)$ = the $i^{th}$ individual base learner
- $M$ = total number of base learners
- $\mathcal{F}(\cdot)$ = the *combiner function* (averaging, voting, weighted sum, etc.)
- $H(x)$ = the final ensemble hypothesis

## 1.2 The Wisdom of Crowds — A Simple Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: The "Panel of Doctors"**
> Imagine you have a complex medical diagnosis. Rather than visiting a single doctor, you visit **10 different specialists** and aggregate their opinions.
> - If each doctor is **70% accurate independently**, you might intuitively think the panel is also 70% accurate.
> - But if their **errors are independent**, the majority of correct diagnoses dominates, and the panel accuracy can climb to **85–95%**.
> - This is the **essence of ensemble learning**: many *moderately accurate*, *diverse* models, when combined, yield a *highly accurate* meta-model.

The two indispensable ingredients of a successful ensemble are:
1. **Accuracy** — Each base learner must perform better than random guessing.
2. **Diversity** — The errors made by each learner must be *uncorrelated* (or at least weakly correlated).

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Ensemble Methods

| Family | Core Strategy | Famous Algorithm | Reduction Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bagging** | Parallel training on bootstrap samples | Random Forest | Variance ↓ |
| **Boosting** | Sequential, error-focused reweighting | AdaBoost, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost | Bias ↓ |
| **Stacking** | Meta-learner over base predictions | Blended Models | Bias + Variance |
| **Voting** | Hard/soft majority aggregation | Hard Voting, Soft Voting | Variance ↓ |

## 1.4 Why Does Ensembling Work? — Bias-Variance Lens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bias-Variance Decomposition (Krogh & Vedelsby, 1995)**
> The squared error of an ensemble can be decomposed as:
> $$E\left[(H(x) - y)^2\right] = \overline{\text{bias}}^2 + \frac{1}{M}\text{Var}(h_i(x)) + \left(1 - \frac{1}{M}\right)\text{Var}_{\text{ambiguity}}$$
> The **ambiguity term** is the variance of individual predictions around the ensemble mean. Ensembles *exploit* ambiguity (diversity) to push total error below that of any single learner.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Bagging — Bootstrap Aggregating

### 2.1.1 Algorithmic Logic Steps

1. Given training dataset $D$ of size $N$.
2. For $b = 1, 2, \ldots, M$ (number of bootstrap iterations):
   - Draw a **bootstrap sample** $D_b$ of size $N$ from $D$ **with replacement**.
   - Train a base learner $h_b(\cdot)$ on $D_b$.
3. Combine predictions:
   - **Regression:** $\hat{f}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{b=1}^{M} h_b(x)$
   - **Classification:** $\hat{y}_{\text{bag}}(x) = \text{mode}\{h_1(x), h_2(x), \ldots, h_M(x)\}$

### 2.1.2 The 'Why' Behind Bagging

- Bootstrap sampling decorrelates the trees.
- Averaging independent estimators reduces variance quadratically with $M$.
- Each sample uses only **~63.2%** unique instances (the rest are duplicates), leaving **~36.8%** as **Out-of-Bag (OOB)** samples for free validation.

## 2.2 Random Forest

Random Forest (Breiman, 2001) extends bagging by introducing **random feature selection** at each split, further decorrelating the trees.

### 2.2.1 Splitting Rule

At each node of tree $b$, Random Forest selects the **best split** only from a **random subset** of $m_{try}$ features drawn from the total $p$ features.

Recommended defaults:
- Classification: $m_{try} = \sqrt{p}$
- Regression: $m_{try} = p/3$

### 2.2.2 Gini Impurity (Splitting Criterion for Classification)

$$Gini(t) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$

Where $p_i$ is the proportion of class $i$ samples at node $t$, and $C$ is the total number of classes. The Gini Gain for a candidate split is:

$$\Delta Gini = Gini(\text{parent}) - \sum_{k \in \{L,R\}} \frac{N_k}{N_{parent}} Gini(k)$$

### 2.2.3 Feature Importance (Mean Decrease in Impurity — MDI)

$$Importance(X_j) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{b=1}^{M}\sum_{t \in T_b : v(t) = X_j} p(t)\cdot \Delta Gini(t)$$

Where $p(t) = N_t / N$ is the proportion of samples reaching node $t$.

## 2.3 Boosting — Sequential Error Correction

Boosting trains learners **sequentially**, with each subsequent learner focused on the mistakes of the previous ones.

### 2.3.1 AdaBoost (Adaptive Boosting) — Key Formulas

For binary classification with labels $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$:

**Step 1 — Initialize weights:** $w_i^{(1)} = 1/N$ for all $i$.

**Step 2 — Train weak learner $h_m$** and compute weighted error:

$$\epsilon_m = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(m)} \cdot \mathbb{1}(h_m(x_i) \neq y_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i^{(m)}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute learner weight:**

$$\alpha_m = \frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{1 - \epsilon_m}{\epsilon_m}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Update sample weights:**

$$w_i^{(m+1)} = w_i^{(m)} \cdot \exp\left(-\alpha_m y_i h_m(x_i)\right)$$

**Step 5 — Final prediction:**

$$H(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{m=1}^{M} \alpha_m h_m(x)\right)$$

### 2.3.2 Gradient Boosting — The Modern Workhorse

Gradient Boosting (Friedman, 2001) generalizes boosting by **fitting each new learner to the negative gradient of a loss function**.

**General Algorithm (Functional Gradient Descent View):**

1. Initialize model with a constant: $F_0(x) = \arg\min_{\gamma} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(y_i, \gamma)$.
2. For $m = 1, 2, \ldots, M$:
   - Compute **pseudo-residuals** (negative gradient of loss):
$$r_{im} = -\left[\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right]_{F = F_{m-1}}$$
   - Fit a base learner $h_m(x)$ to predict $r_{im}$ from $x_i$.
   - Compute the optimal step size via line search:
$$\gamma_m = \arg\min_{\gamma} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\left(y_i, F_{m-1}(x_i) + \gamma h_m(x_i)\right)$$
   - Update the ensemble:
$$F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu \cdot \gamma_m h_m(x)$$
Where $\nu \in (0,1]$ is the **learning rate** (shrinkage factor).

### 2.3.3 Common Loss Functions and Their Negative Gradients

| Loss Function $L(y, F)$ | Domain | Negative Gradient $r_{im}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Squared Error (Regression) | $\frac{1}{2}(y_i - F(x_i))^2$ | $y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i)$ |
| Absolute Error (Regression) | $\vert y_i - F(x_i) \vert$ | $\text{sign}(y_i - F_{m-1}(x_i))$ |
| Binomial Deviance (Classification) | $\ln(1 + e^{-2y_i F(x_i)})$ | $\frac{2y_i}{1 + e^{2y_i F_{m-1}(x_i)}}$ |
| Multi-class Deviance | Cross-entropy | Residual = $y_{ik} - p_{k,m-1}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> - **Random Forest:** Used in medical diagnostics, credit scoring, remote sensing, and bioinformatics where robustness to noise and feature interpretability (via importance scores) are paramount.
> - **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost):** Dominates Kaggle competitions and is deployed in production at Microsoft Bing, Uber ETA prediction, fraud detection at PayPal, and recommendation systems at Netflix.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Purpose / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ensemble combiner (Regression) | $H(x) = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{b=1}^{M} h_b(x)$ | Bagging final prediction |
| 2 | Ensemble combiner (Classification) | $H(x) = \text{mode}\{h_1(x), \ldots, h_M(x)\}$ | Hard majority voting |
| 3 | Gini Impurity | $Gini(t) = 1 - \sum_{i} p_i^2$ | RF splitting criterion |
| 4 | Entropy | $H(t) = -\sum_{i} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Alternative split criterion |
| 5 | AdaBoost learner weight | $\alpha_m = \frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{1-\epsilon_m}{\epsilon_m}\right)$ | Voting weight |
| 6 | AdaBoost weight update | $w_i^{(m+1)} = w_i^{(m)} e^{-\alpha_m y_i h_m(x_i)}$ | Sample reweighting |
| 7 | Gradient Boosting residual | $r_{im} = -\frac{\partial L}{\partial F}\big\vert_{F_{m-1}}$ | Pseudo-target for new tree |
| 8 | Gradient Boosting update | $F_m = F_{m-1} + \nu \gamma_m h_m(x)$ | Iterative ensemble build |
| 9 | OOB Error Estimate | $E_{OOB} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}(H_{OOB}(x_i) \neq y_i)$ | Free validation metric |
| 10 | Bias-Variance Trade-off | $E[(H-y)^2] = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance} + \text{Noise}$ | Error decomposition |

> [!IMPORTANT]

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Averaging Reduces Variance (Bagging Justification)

**Given:** $M$ independent base learners, each with variance $\sigma^2$.

**Find:** The variance of the averaged prediction $\bar{h} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M} h_m$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

The variance of a sum of independent random variables equals the sum of variances:

$$
\text{Var}\left(\sum_{m=1}^{M} h_m\right) = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \text{Var}(h_m) = M \cdot \sigma^2
$$

Dividing by $M^2$ (variance of a constant times a random variable):

$$
\text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M} h_m\right) = \frac{1}{M^2} \cdot M \cdot \sigma^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{M}
$$

**Conclusion:** Averaging $M$ independent estimators reduces variance by a factor of $M$. This is the **theoretical foundation of bagging**.

**Realistic refinement:** If learners are correlated with pairwise correlation $\rho$:

$$
\text{Var}\left(\frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M} h_m\right) = \rho \sigma^2 + \frac{1-\rho}{M} \sigma^2
$$

> This is precisely why **Random Forest injects feature randomness** — to reduce $\rho$ and push variance reduction toward the ideal $\sigma^2/M$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Gradient Boosting as Functional Gradient Descent

We want to minimize the empirical risk:

$$
J(F) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} L(y_i, F(x_i))
$$

Treat $F(x)$ as a function in an infinite-dimensional hypothesis space. Apply **steepest descent** in function space:

$$
F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) - \eta_m \cdot \left[\nabla_F J(F)\right]_{F=F_{m-1}}
$$

Where the gradient with respect to $F(x_i)$ is:

$$
\frac{\partial J(F)}{\partial F(x_i)} = \frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}
$$

The negative gradient direction at training point $i$ is:

$$
r_{im} = -\left[\frac{\partial L(y_i, F(x_i))}{\partial F(x_i)}\right]_{F=F_{m-1}}
$$

Since we cannot perform a true infinite-dim step, we **restrict the step to lie in the span of a base learner** $h_m(x)$ (e.g., a regression tree). This is **stagewise additive modeling**, and the optimal step $\gamma_m$ is found by line search:

$$
\gamma_m = \arg\min_{\gamma} \sum_{i=1}^{N} L\left(y_i, F_{m-1}(x_i) + \gamma h_m(x_i)\right)
$$

With shrinkage $\nu \in (0, 1]$ to control overfitting:

$$
F_m(x) = F_{m-1}(x) + \nu \cdot \gamma_m h_m(x)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the deepest KTU takeaway:** Gradient Boosting is simply **gradient descent in function space**, where the "model parameters" are the function values $F(x_i)$ themselves. Every KTU question that asks "Why does GB work?" can be answered with this identity.

## 3.3 Worked Example: AdaBoost on 5 Toy Points

Suppose $D = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{5}$ with $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, initialized with $w_i^{(1)} = 0.2$.

**Iteration 1:** Train $h_1$. Suppose it misclassifies 1 of 5 → $\epsilon_1 = 0.2$.

$$
\alpha_1 = \frac{1}{2}\ln\left(\frac{1-0.2}{0.2}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\ln(4) \approx 0.693
$$

For **misclassified points** (3 points, $y_i h_1(x_i) = -1$):

$$
w_i^{(2)} = 0.2 \cdot e^{-0.693 \cdot (-1)} = 0.2 \cdot e^{0.693} = 0.2 \cdot 2 = 0.4
$$

For **correctly classified points** (2 points, $y_i h_1(x_i) = +1$):

$$
w_i^{(2)} = 0.2 \cdot e^{-0.693 \cdot (1)} = 0.2 \cdot 0.5 = 0.1
$$

**Normalization check:** Sum = $3(0.4) + 2(0.1) = 1.4$. Divide all by 1.4 → misclassified = $0.286$, correct = $0.071$.

**Observation:** The misclassified sample's weight increased from 0.2 → 0.286 (43% boost), forcing the next learner to focus on it. This is the **error-correction mechanism** of boosting.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Bagging vs. Boosting

| Dimension | Bagging (Random Forest) | Boosting (Gradient Boosting) |
|---|---|---|
| **Training** | Parallel, independent | Sequential, dependent |
| **Primary Error Reduced** | Variance | Bias |
| **Base Learners** | Deep, unpruned trees | Shallow trees (stumps, depth 3-8) |
| **Weighting** | Equal vote / average | Weighted by accuracy |
| **Sensitivity to Noise** | Robust | Sensitive (overfits outliers) |
| **Interpretability** | High (feature importance) | Medium (SHAP needed) |
| **Training Speed** | Fast, parallelizable | Slow, sequential |
| **Risk of Overfitting** | Low (even with $M \to \infty$) | Higher (controlled by $\nu$, $M$) |
| **Best For** | High-noise data, high-dim features | Low-noise data, competitions |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Architecture: Random Forest Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startA[Input Training Data D of Size N] --> bag1[Bootstrap Sample D1]
    startA --> bag2[Bootstrap Sample D2]
    startA --> bag3[Bootstrap Sample D3]
    startA --> bagM[Bootstrap Sample DM]

    bag1 --> t1[Tree 1 with Random Feature Subset]
    bag2 --> t2[Tree 2 with Random Feature Subset]
    bag3 --> t3[Tree 3 with Random Feature Subset]
    bagM --> tM[Tree M with Random Feature Subset]

    t1 --> pred1[Prediction h1 of x]
    t2 --> pred2[Prediction h2 of x]
    t3 --> pred3[Prediction h3 of x]
    tM --> predM[Prediction hM of x]

    pred1 --> agg[Aggregator: Voting or Averaging]
    pred2 --> agg
    pred3 --> agg
    predM --> agg

    agg --> finalA[Final Ensemble Output H of x]

    oob1[OOB Samples of D1] -.-> score1[OOB Score 1]
    oob2[OOB Samples of D2] -.-> score2[OOB Score 2]
    oob3[OOB Samples of D3] -.-> score3[OOB Score 3]
    oobM[OOB Samples of DM] -.-> scoreM[OOB Score M]

    score1 --> oobAgg[OOB Error Estimation]
    score2 --> oobAgg
    score3 --> oobAgg
    scoreM --> oobAgg

    oobAgg --> finalA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence: Gradient Boosting Iterative Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    initA[Initialize F0 of x as constant] --> loopStart{For m equals 1 to M}
    loopStart --> resStep[Compute Negative Gradients r_im]
    resStep --> fitStep[Fit Base Learner h_m to r_im]
    fitStep --> gammaStep[Line Search for Optimal gamma_m]
    gammaStep --> updateStep[Update F_m = F_{m-1} + nu times gamma_m times h_m]
    updateStep --> loopStart

    loopStart --> doneA[Output Final Model F_M of x]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Comparison: Bagging vs Boosting Topology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph baggingGroup[Bagging Parallel Training]
        data1[Original Data D] --> b1[Bootstrap D1] --> model1[Model 1]
        data1 --> b2[Bootstrap D2] --> model2[Model 2]
        data1 --> b3[Bootstrap D3] --> model3[Model 3]
        model1 --> voteA[Majority Vote]
        model2 --> voteA
        model3 --> voteA
    end

    subgraph boostingGroup[Boosting Sequential Training]
        d1[Data D Weights w1] --> m1[Model 1] --> up1[Update Weights w2]
        up1 --> d2[Data D Weights w2] --> m2[Model 2] --> up2[Update Weights w3]
        up2 --> d3[Data D Weights w3] --> m3[Model 3]
        m1 --> weightedSum[Weighted Sum F_M of x]
        m2 --> weightedSum
        m3 --> weightedSum
    end
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Architecture: AdaBoost Weight Update Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inputA[Initialize Weights w_i equals 1 over N] --> train1[Train Weak Learner h1]
    train1 --> err1[Compute Weighted Error epsilon_1]
    err1 --> alpha1[Compute Learner Weight alpha_1]
    alpha1 --> up1[Update Sample Weights w_i^{m+1}]
    up1 --> train2[Train Weak Learner h2 on Updated Weights]
    train2 --> err2[Compute Weighted Error epsilon_2]
    err2 --> alpha2[Compute Learner Weight alpha_2]
    alpha2 --> up2[Update Sample Weights]
    up2 --> trainM[Train Weak Learner hM]
    trainM --> combine[Final H of x equals sign of sum alpha_m h_m of x]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `bag1`, `t1`, `finalA`), no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names, and all node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown bold/italic tags, as mandated by the protocol.
